> I'm assuming that we would want to open all file for append

Here is the commit history details regarding the licensing terms:

- Commit f6f4aab6 (2012-08-17) is the initial commit that shows that the project was originally licensed under GNU LGPL.
- Commits b1b10d14 (2013-05-21), 868b5337 (2013-06-06) and dcb35e46 (2013-08-07) mention BSD license.
- Commit a6ce4655 (2013-06-06) shows the intention of moving from GNU LGPL to BSD license.
- Commit 4f13f63a (2014-06-30) mentions GNU LGPL in the classifiers metadata.

In the latest `master` branch (at commit c38d598):

- Files `LICENSE` and `setup.py` mention GNU LGPL only.
- Files `docs/index.rst`, `pylama/libs/inirama.py`, and `README.rst` mention BSD license only.
- File `pylama/__init__.py` mentions both BSD license and GNU LGPL.

The project was originally licensed under GNU LGPL but later the author appears to have an intention of moving to BSD license. While there are several commits that provide evidence in favour of this conclusion, commit 4f13f63a contradicts this conclusion. The only place in the project that attempts to communicate the license explicitly to users or readers is `README.rst` which mentions:

> Licensed under a BSD license.

It is unclear which BSD license (BSD-3-Clause or BSD-2-Clause) is meant here.

Here is Debian's interpretation of the licensing terms (quoting from `/usr/share/doc/pylama/copyright` of Debian GNU/Linux 9 (stretch)):

Objects implementing multiple interfaces are a bit of a complicated case. There are cases like MPRIS2, where all methods are grouped into 4 interfaces, and 3 of them are extensions; and there are cases where they're used for versioning, or dynamically added and removed based on some events (like a optical drive, that adds an interface when a CD is inserted).

In the MPRIS case, I guess annotating decorators are the way to go. The other two cases aren't currently supported by pydbus in any sane way, so they're out of scope here.

So, we have two ways to specify MPRIS's ActivatePlaylist method:

1.
```
@dbus_method(("o"), interface="org.mpris.MediaPlayer2.Playlists")
def ActivatePlaylist(self, playlist_path):
	...
```
2.
```
@interface("org.mpris.MediaPlayer2.Playlists")
@typed_method(("o"))
def ActivatePlaylist(self, playlist_path):
	...
```

I feel the second one is prettier, as we're stating two different facts about the method; and the "at interface" sounds semantically well.

The @interface decorator of course can be used also for properties and signals.

--------------------------

However, while thinking about it more, I've found another possibility:

3.
```
@interface("org.mpris.MediaPlayer2.Playlists")
def ActivatePlaylist(self, playlist_path: "o"):
	...
```

And this is the cleanest option. However: Python 2 does not support annotations :D But while we could provide a Python 2 fallback (2nd option, with @typed_method renamed to @annotate_method, and without type checking), this is a place where we're clearly using a Python 3 feature, and we can direct Python 2 users to the existing, XML file based method of specifying interfaces. And I feel there're already too many hacks for Python 2 in pydbus, and it's time to give people reasons to migrate to Python 3.

In the future, @typechecked decorator could be added to verify the annotations at runtime, but it's not really required now, and outside of scope of this feature.

Also, as it's really easy to annotate methods this way, I don't think that require_strong_typing=False is useful anymore - we can always throw an exception when a unannotated method is found.

> This will not give back control until ALL fd is send through the socket. 

Just to clarify, when using sendfile() with a non-blocking socket sendfile() does not send the whole file in one shot. It sends a chunk of it (typically 65k) even if you specify a bigger buffer size, then it returns the number of bytes being sent (e.g. see [pyftpdlib implementation](https://github.com/giampaolo/pyftpdlib/blob/6165a40fd4f7c7cd2d549dbb816b3208091ffdff/pyftpdlib/handlers.py#L658-L682)).
The actual blocking operation is the "internal file read()". If the socket fd is not "ready to be written" sendfile() immediately returns EAGAIN. So really it's an hybrid: the socket fd part is non blocking, the file fd part is blocking.

As to whether the read() part is "fast enough" I currently have no documentation to back this up so that should indeed be investigated. My point was trying to provide an API flexible enough so that you can choose which approach to use (threads vs. non-threads), depending on how much you trust your disk speed.

Hi!

`addict` does not differentiate between attributes and and items (except when it comes to attributes that belong to a superclass such as `dict`, those can not be set again). That means that the example above is valid in the following form as well;

```Python
d = addict.Dict()
if d.this_key_is_not_present:
    print('this doesn't actually happen')
```

In fact, I don't think it's possible to implement the nested setting of attributes, i.e.
```Python
d = addict.Dict()
d.a.b.c = 2
```
without returning a `addict.Dict` instance on a `__getattr__` call. I encourage you to take a look at the source in, but I'm of course also open to be proven wrong!

Hope you have a great weekend, and thanks for contributing to `addict`.

Original comment by: Jason R. Coombs

The password that unlocks the keyring is only used in keyrings.alt in the encrypted file keyring, which is considered deprecated because of its security implications.

Keyrings.alt could do what you propose, but it would require storing the encryption password somewhere in plaintext on the disk (how else could it be achieved?).

My recommendation is that you rely on the recommended keyrings in keyring, which rely on system credential vaults and their integrated security implementations. If you really feel like this feature is something that keyrings should support, I recommend implementing it as a third-party library and demonstrating the technique. At that point, perhaps we can consider adopting it in the library, especially if it solves these issues above.

An easier workaround might have been to simply set `LOCALAPPDATA="C:\Program Files (x86)\Apache Software Foundation\Apache2.2\conf"` in the environment in which the Apache service runs.

Still, keyring could do better here.

Thanks for clarifications.  This is all sounding O. K. too me as far as it goes, but…

I've just realized why I hadn't been bitten by the change as @dalcinl  was this time, whereas I had been back in #169.  I don't ask meshio to write .msh files, only read them, and that implicitly by using `pygmsh.generate_mesh`; the hidden temporary .msh files being written by Gmsh in a `subprocess`.  Thus far, I'm working directly with the five objects returned by `generate_mesh` (i.e. by `meshio.read`) and haven't needed to serialize the mesh; if I did, and chose `meshio.write` and Gmsh .msh for the purpose, I would have hit this problem, yes.  I would certainly have expected the physical tagging written by pygmsh and Gmsh into the `$Element` block to have persisted there.

So this isn't really about what I thought it was when this issue was launched in December; i.e. whether the data structure for the regions should be inverted.  This sounds more like #197, but I [see](https://github.com/nschloe/meshio/issues/197#issuecomment-361639216) that the discussion from there was moved back here.

To return it for the moment to the original question of which way the looking-up of regions should go, I see that Gmsh in the MSH format in the `$Element` block stores it as element -> region.  And if it were in `$ElementData` it would still be nearly the same: elm-number -> region, 'elm-number' being the index into the list or array of elements.  This means that if I only want to integrate over a region, I need to filter either of those look-ups to get the elements or elm-numbers that lie in the region of interest.  The intention of the inverse was to have dicts going the other way: region -> elm-number, so that such a traversal would be more direct.  I believe, as mentioned [above](https://github.com/nschloe/meshio/issues/175#issuecomment-353665284), that this is what Exodus's 'element blocks' do, but studying the spec for [Gmsh MSH](http://gmsh.info/doc/texinfo/gmsh.html#MSH-ASCII-file-format) again, I can't see any way to encode a mapping in that direction without inverting it.  It also sounds like VTK has no support for region -> elm-number either.  Is Exodus on its own?  Anyway, without support in Gmsh or VTK, it's maybe not worth having inverting the look-up read from the `$Element` block on the way in as one is just going to have to undo that on the way out in most cases.

First note that `Tuple[(int, ...)]` is no valid Python 2.7 code and pytypes assumes that type comments contain code that is compatible with the currently running interpreter (try to process `test_type = Tuple[(int, ...)]` in ordinary Python 2.7 code). I thought this is in line with PEP 484, because they demand all imports and locals/globals (e.g. type variables) required by code in type comments. Type comments are like usual code, just in a special position.

That said, I would actually like this to work anyway. I'm thinking of some text-based preprocessing hack that allows this to work on Python 2.7. I cannot promise now that I will be able to come up with something totally reasonable. We'll see...

The current implementation of `rename_keys()` *does* return a shallow copy (i.e. a new dict object is returned whose top-level keys can be modified without affecting the original):

```python
>>> from pydash import rename_keys
>>> obj = {'a': {'b': 1, 'c': 2}, 'd': 1}
>>> renamed = rename_keys(obj, {})
>>> renamed is obj
False
>>> for key in list(renamed.keys()): del renamed[key]
...
>>> renamed
{}
>>> obj
{'a': {'b': 1, 'c': 2}, 'd': 1}
>>> renamed = rename_keys(obj, {})
>>> for key in renamed: renamed[key] = True
...
>>> renamed
{'a': True, 'd': True}
>>> obj
{'a': {'b': 1, 'c': 2}, 'd': 1}
```

This is similar behavior as doing `renamed = obj.copy()`.

Dear all,

my impression is that we should just drop bss_eval_sources. There is a lot of controversy about that one, that actually compute scores based on a filtered version of the true sources, hence modifying the reference signal depending on its estimation. 

My feeling is that the source separation field is anyway going in the direction of stereo signals and that evaluating image separation is sufficient there. Indeed, the problem of evaluating a stereo-to-mono method that would go from the image to the original mono source is in my opinion a somewhat different problem

Hence, I would advise to just stick to bss_eval_images there

I would also be happy with option 1 and call it a feature :) Like you said, adding another option to `saveas()` won't really fix the other issues, and mine is already fixed with the available options. 

My thinking is that  `.saveas()` is expected to be a `BedTool` object, whereas `output` would be the end of that chain of operations by a BED function. So there is already:
a) in-built **robustness** using `.saveas()` which makes sure that the obj is `BedTool`, and 
b) **flexibility** because one can ignore proper bed format and save whatever the result is with `output=`.

Adding a line explaining the difference between `output=`and `.saveas()` in the documentation should suffice.

I will like to add a little information here about jedi-vim so it can be taken into account when anyone starts working on this.

I have been using this https://github.com/nakamuray/jedi-rpc.vim to fix all my problems related to Python+virtualenv version compatibility problems and package discovery in jedi-vim. All credit goes to @nakamuray , of course.

What this little Vim module does is intercepting the jedi-vim interface and run all jedi calls in the current system's python. In other words: If you launch Vim inside an activated Python virtual environment, you can use all the options in jedi-vim with whatever version of Python you have in the venv. The internal Python inside Vim is not used for anything related to jedi or jedi-vim, just by the jedi-rpc adapter.

What I wanted to say is that to keep supporting this kind of schemes it will be nice if the server is able to be launched as a in-environment process. This will allow it to be sensible to whatever tools and libraries can be found in the environment.

I guess jedi already uses a Python 2+3 approach to cover a many cases as possible. If we keep this jedi will be able to adapt completely to the environments.

We can event add support for https://github.com/pypa/pipenv or `.env` files.

Your thoughts about this?

Uhm... yes, using `stat.S_IF*` functions looks like a good idea (and the doc should mention them).
Maybe we should have an extra "type" which will be a human readable string like "regular", "fifo", etc. 
It must be noted that `stat.S_IF*` functions do not satisfy all possible types though (there are more) so they are not enough in order to fill all the possible "type" fields. 

"position" (offset) only makes sense for regular files. I see that `lsof` has a column called "SIZE/OFF(set)"  so I suppose that depending on the fd sometimes it refers to it's size (e.g. a directory - 4096), some other times to its offset/position (e.g. a regular file). As such maybe our field should be called differently ("sizeoff" probably).    

`lsof` also has a NODE column which should be the inode. AFAIU that's needed in order to identify a fd uniquely so it looks like it's something we want to provide as well. 

"path" field no longer makes sense as a fd can be a socket or something else different than a path. `lsof` call this "NAME", which looks like a better name. 

`open_pipes()` and `open_special()` are probably useless. I envision this in form of a single `open_fds(type=None)` method where the type argument is probably a string ("regular", "pipe", "fifo", etc).

In summary, I envision this as something along these lines:

```
>>> p.open_fds()
[popenfile(name='/tmp/foo', fd=4, sizeoff=292, flags=32768, node=2, type="regular"),
 ...]
```

One thing which leaves me puzzled is how socket fds should be represented, because they have extra information (local / remote address and status). Right now we already have `Process.connections()` which covers this use case, so we risk to duplicate functionality. FWIW `lsof` puts local/remote address and states in the NAME column.

*"This is only really a problem on multi-user systems *nix systems. If an
unauthorized third party is able to read files on your system, your discord
tokens being stolen will be the least of your problems."*

What a particularly odd thing to say.  Leaving authentication information
in a file in /tmp that is potentially world readable because the software
creating it does nothing to secure it is just pure laziness.  Rationalizing
that behavior by saying that it's the least of someone's problems is just
plain ignorant.  Would the glib response be the same if someone were
talking about your computer account, email account, Facebook or other
social media account?  Multiple users having access to the same files is
the hallmark of a multiuser system, whether it's *unix or Windows or any
other OS.

Having the software itself open and write the file securely in Python is
extremely easy and any piece of software should take steps to be as secure
as possible, especially with user's authentication information.  Yes, the
user can change their umask.  But any good programmer knows you don't rely
on the user to properly execute and protect the environment, especially
where security is concerned.  If we did, we wouldn't need to encrypt
passwords.

I highly appreciate all the work that the folks working on Python's discord
API library do, but I couldn't in good conscience read that response and
not respond with dismayed surprise.

Thanks for getting in touch. I would definitely be open to patches which made a measurable improvement to performance. So far I haven't put effort into micro-optimising the code for two reasons:
1. In performance sensitive contexts most people run whitenoise behind a cache so improvements to whitenoise only affect the cache-miss response time, which usually isn't a priority.
2. Whitenoise is designed to do as much work as possible on initialisation, so there's actually not much code on the hot path. The response time is most likely to be dominated by time spent in the server (gunicon, waitress etc.) and in Django itself (if you're using it) rather than in Whitenoise.

Once we have the `StaticFile` instance (which in production mode is just a lookup in the `files` dict) all the work is done in the [serve](https://github.com/evansd/whitenoise/blob/1266e8c44264f34a6920fc75085f3207e0ab14b6/whitenoise/base.py#L75) method, and most of the time there is in calling [get_response](https://github.com/evansd/whitenoise/blob/1266e8c44264f34a6920fc75085f3207e0ab14b6/whitenoise/responders.py#L41) on the `StaticFile`.

The Range header handling complicates things a bit but that's a rare case and most requests can be served pretty simply.

In terms of the API, none of this is officially documented but I'd say that the `get_response` method signature is semi-public in that some people have produced alternative integrations that use it so that interface should be preserved. Anything else can be changed if necessary.

Hey @biocar2001!

I was just looking at your code, and I think you can use the internal [pager variables](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#pager-variables) to accomplish the same thing.

Like I said, I don't know knockout, but you can access any of the internal pager variables from `table.config.pager`. From here you have access to:

* `pager.startRow` - one-based index of the first row visible in the pager.
* `pager.endRow` - one-based index of the last row visible in the pager.
* `pager.page` - currently selected page
* `pager.size` - currently selected page size.
* `pager.totalPages` - total pages determined by the current page size.
* `pager.totalRows` - total rows within the table, or returned by ajax.
* `pager.filteredPages` - filtered pages accessible by the pager. This equals `totalPages` if no filters are applied.
* `pager.filteredRows` - filtered rows accessible by pager. This equals `totalRows` if no filters are applied.
* `pager.$container` - pager outer wrapper (jQuery object)
* `pager.$goto` - pager page selector (jQuery object)
* `pager.$size` - pager size selector (jQuery object)

Then to set the page and/or size, use any of the available [pager methods](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#methods-pager).

I'm with stennie on this one, I use the following to triage log files and would like to see the progress in the console, without tee'ing it to the file:
```
        mloginfo --distinct  --restarts --queries --connections --rsstate $file | tee ${d}/${f}.distinct
````

Note: the buffering of tee with the flushes to stderr  means that the progress bar comes out before the final block of text, so I get exactly what I want.

Changing update_progress in  cmdline.py seems to fix this for me (even on a MAC!)

```
    def update_progress(self, progress, prefix=''):
        """ use this helper function to print a progress bar for longer-running scripts.
            The progress value is a value between 0.0 and 1.0. If a prefix is present, it
            will be printed before the progress bar.
        """
        total_length = 40

        if progress == 1.:
            sys.stderr.write('\r' + ' '*(total_length + len(prefix) + 50))
            sys.stderr.write('\n')
            sys.stderr.flush()
        else:
            bar_length = int(round(total_length*progress))
            sys.stderr.write('\r%s [%s%s] %.1f %% ' % (prefix, '='*bar_length, ' '*(total_length-bar_length), progress*100))
            sys.stderr.flush()
```

If you look in the code, you'll see that `Presentation` is actually a function https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/blob/master/pptx/api.py#L20.

The class that ultimately gets constructed is `pptx.presentation.Presentation`. That constructor takes a reference to the presentation part, and might not work properly without it, depending on what calls you make, possibly including `.save()`. So you'll need to have a closer look and see if that's going to work for you.

These classes aren't really designed to be superclasses, so you might want to consider composition instead if you want to build on them somehow.

```
## AO3 offers a page to download the entire work at once.  This can
## save a lot of network traffic if there are many chapters.
## True by built-in default.
#use_view_full_work:true
```
AO3 downloads by default download the entire story in one network request.  ffnet does not offer a full work page and must be fetched one chapter at a time.

If you are updating to add a single chapter to an existing epub, ffnet download might be quicker because the `use_view_full_work` setting requires the AO3 server to collect all the chapters even if they all come in one request.  But it might not--as I recall, ffnet downloads need more requests to collect all the metadata.

I had some problem with script finding `ffmpeg.exe`. It was present in `c:\python27` but the script just kept on raising error which said it had not found the `ffmpeg.exe`. So I modified `extract_audio` function on line 135 throuth 145 in `autosub_app.py` like this: 
```
def extract_audio(filename, channels=1, rate=16000):
    temp = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix='.wav', delete=False)
    if not os.path.isfile(filename):
        print "The given file does not exist: {0}".format(filename)
        raise Exception("Invalid filepath: {0}".format(filename))
    #if not which("ffmpeg"):
    #    print "ffmpeg: Executable not found on machine."
    #    raise Exception("Dependency not found: ffmpeg")
    command = ["ffmpeg.exe", "-y", "-i", filename, "-ac", str(channels), "-ar", str(rate), "-loglevel", "error", temp.name]
    subprocess.check_output(command)
    return temp.name, rate
```

It seems to me that the chunk API is not finished yet. There's no documentation on the website yet, and 1.0.26 isn't mentioned in the news or changelog on the website. Also, chunk information is not available for all file formats and all chunk types.

The more important issue is that I want PySoundFile to be a library for reading/writing sound files in Python, and not necessarily a complete set of bindings for libsndfile. Hi Mark,

I just committed a change that both:

1. Makes the enhancement requested here -- the only thing to watch for here is that if you add something to a _keyless_ list, then the behaviour is that you get the key back still (whereas for lists with a key it returns the item).

1. Fixes the issue with using spaces in keys. This is done by specifying add(keyname=keyval) - a concatenated string representation is still used for getitem() - since this is backwards compatible, but a new _item() function is added such that you can retrieve items using the same logic. I don't want to change the existing ```__getitem__``` behaviour because this will make things less nice for cases where there is only a single key.

HARFBUZZ_URL = HARFBUZZ_HOST + HARFBUZZ_TARBALL


def cmd(string, cwd=None):
    subprocess.run(string, shell=True, check=True, cwd=cwd)


def download(url, target_path):
    try:
        from urllib.request import urlretrieve
    except ImportError:
        from urllib import urlretrieve

    print("Downloading {}...".format(url))
    urlretrieve(url, target_path)


root_dir = "."
prefix_dir = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(root_dir, "local"))
os.makedirs(prefix_dir, exist_ok=True)
build_dir = os.path.join(root_dir, "build")
build_dir_ft = os.path.join(build_dir,
                            FREETYPE_TARBALL.split(".tar")[0], "build")
build_dir_hb = os.path.join(build_dir,
                            HARFBUZZ_TARBALL.split(".tar")[0], "build")
os.makedirs(build_dir, exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs(build_dir_ft, exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs(build_dir_hb, exist_ok=True)

ft_tarball = os.path.join(build_dir, FREETYPE_TARBALL)
if not os.path.exists(ft_tarball):
    download(FREETYPE_URL, ft_tarball)

    with tarfile.open(ft_tarball, 'r:bz2') as tb:
        tb.extractall(build_dir)

hb_tarball = os.path.join(build_dir, HARFBUZZ_TARBALL)
if not os.path.exists(hb_tarball):
    download(HARFBUZZ_URL, hb_tarball)

    with tarfile.open(hb_tarball, 'r:bz2') as tb:
        tb.extractall(build_dir)

os.putenv("CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH", prefix_dir)

print("\n# First, build FreeType without Harfbuzz support")
cmd("cmake -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS:BOOL=false "
    "-DWITH_HarfBuzz=OFF -DWITH_PNG=OFF -DWITH_BZip2=OFF -DWITH_ZLIB=OFF "
    "-DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=\"{}\" ..".format(prefix_dir),
    cwd=build_dir_ft)
cmd("cmake --build . --config Release --target install", cwd=build_dir_ft)

print("\n# Next, build Harfbuzz and point it to the FreeType we just build.")
cmd(
    "cmake -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS:BOOL=false "
    "-DCMAKE_POSITION_INDEPENDENT_CODE=ON "  # Needed on Linux for next step.
    "-DHB_HAVE_FREETYPE=ON -DHB_HAVE_GLIB=OFF "
    "-DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=\"{}\" ..".format(prefix_dir),
    cwd=build_dir_hb)
cmd("cmake --build . --config Release --target install", cwd=build_dir_hb)

print("\n# Lastly, rebuild FreeType, this time with Harfbuzz support.")
cmd("cmake -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS:BOOL=true -DMINGW=ON "
    "-DWITH_HarfBuzz=ON -DWITH_PNG=OFF -DWITH_BZip2=OFF -DWITH_ZLIB=OFF "
    "-DSKIP_INSTALL_HEADERS=ON "
    "-DHARFBUZZ_INCLUDE_DIRS=\"{}\" "
    "-DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=\"{}\" ..".format(
        os.path.join(prefix_dir, "include", "harfbuzz"), prefix_dir),
    cwd=build_dir_ft)
cmd("cmake --build . --config Release --target install", cwd=build_dir_ft)
```

Unfortunately there are reasons why it needs to work this way. Or rather, why the way you've go there doesn't quite work as it stands.

The problem is that it means that lists that were drawn because they exceeded the max size can't be shrunk even if it would be legitimate to do so, because there's no zero terminator to tell it to stop the list.

A thing that might work that I've been considering is to draw an enforced zero terminator at the end. You could do something like the following after the loop:

```python
if len(result) == self.max_size:
    assume(data.draw_bytes(1, lambda random, n: b'\0') == b'\0')
```

This would then get inserted as the terminator of the list if you the shrinker deletes anything earlier.

Not sure if this is what you're looking for, but you can register a listener for new 'cast' (receiver) status updates ??

In the code you linked to, you can add
```python
def __init__(self, device):
    self.device = device
    self.thumbnail = ""
    self.sendDeviceStatus()
    self.device.media_controller.register_status_listener(self)
    self.device.register_status_listener(self)  # Register for receiver status updates.

def new_cast_status(self, new_status):
""" Called when a new status received from the Chromecast. """
    if new_status.app_id != "CC1AD845":
        pass  # Your code to turn off your speakers can go here.
```

Note that you may want to launch the app on the receiver BEFORE registering the cast/receiver status listener. Otherwise you might end up turning off your speakers before you've started playing anything due to the status being updated for whatever reason. Hope that helps.

P.s. There's also a listener for Network Connection Status updates as well:
```python
def __init__(self, device)
    self.device.register_connection_listener(self)

def new_connection_status(self, new_status):
    # new_status.status will be one of the CONNECTION_STATUS_ constants defined in the
    # socket_client module.
    if new_status.status == CONNECTION_STATUS_DISCONNECTED:
        pass  # Your code to turn off your speakers here.
```

Well, we don't really have the choice. Select are using string as value. From [Angular doc](https://docs.angularjs.org/api/ng/directive/select):

> Note that the value of a select directive used without ngOptions is always a string. When the model needs to be bound to a non-string value, you must either explicitly convert it using a directive (see example below) or use ngOptions to specify the set of options. This is because an option element can only be bound to string values at present.

Moreover, if you try to set this value with a non string, this s what happens (still from [Angular doc](https://docs.angularjs.org/api/ng/directive/select)):

> If the viewValue of ngModel does not match any of the options, then the control will automatically add an "unknown" option, which it then removes when the mismatch is resolved.


For integerField, if you try to enter a string, you'll get an error. We've done what we can on our side for that I think (except the remaining bug, see opened issues). Unless you can think of a better way to handle this problem, I think it does the job.

I was thinking about this in a slightly different way: there is a use case for parsing non-string types from strings. Normally, when used with JSON, JSON parser parses strings into numbers so lollipop numeric types get numbers. However, there is a case when no pre-parser available (e.g. parsing query string parameters) so it would be nice to have a mode/types that can tolerate that.

Although this is not the same as allowing either representation (as is here). What I propose is to introduce parameter like `as_string=True` or `stringified=True` which will change behavor of non-string types (e.g. numbers or booleans) to parse & generate strings instead.

@RyanBalfanz For the example, I shouldn't have named the states the same as the functions. That's just incidental, and the functions could be named whatever you'd want.

When you do

```
state.transition = 'one_way'
```

The idea is the framework locks the system into **only** executing functions decorated with
 
```
@state.one_way
```

In order to 'break out' of that state, you'd need to 

```
state.transition = 'confirm'
```

That way, the same intent can be used in different contexts depending on where you are in the conversation.

Hi,
I made an additional investigation of the export process and here is my results. The main conclusion is - we should take into account what channels are **Active** and what channels are asked to **Export**. Only this way we can choose the right combination of options. 

NOTE: Tested only on my Saleae Logic Pro 8 with 1.2.12 software version.

## `export_data2()` results relative to enabled and exported channels

| Enabled channels | Exported channels | Correct command                          |
| ---------------- | ----------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Analog           | Analog            | ' ' (None)                               |
|                  | Digital           | Wrong combination                        |
|                  | Both              | Wrong combination                        |
|                  |                   |                                          |
| Digital          | Analog            | Wrong combination                        |
|                  | Digital           | ' ' (None)                               |
|                  | Both              | Wrong combination                        |
|                  |                   |                                          |
| Both             | Analog            | ANALOG_ONLY (ANALOG_AND_DIGITAL also works) |
|                  | Digital           | DIGITAL_ONLY                             |
|                  | Both              | ANALOG_AND_DIGITAL                       |


## Examples

**Analog** enabled and **analog** exported:
```python
s.set_active_channels([], [2, 3])
s.export_data2(filename, digital_channels=None, analog_channels=[3])
```
**Digital** enabled and **digital** exported:
```python
s.set_active_channels([0, 1], [])
s.export_data2(filename, digital_channels=[0], analog_channels=None)
```

**Both** enabled
```python
s.set_active_channels([0, 1], [2, 3])
```
and:
- **analog** exported:
  ```python
  s.export_data2(filename, digital_channels=None, analog_channels=[3])
  ```

- **digital** exported:

  ```python
  s.export_data2(filename, digital_channels=[0], analog_channels=None)
  ```

- **both** exported
  ``` python
  s.export_data2(filename, digital_channels=[0], analog_channels=[3])
  ```

@adamchainz This is django-ratelimit 1.1.0 of course. It's one of the functions to compute key values.

> User-Agent may actually be useful if its combined with the IP address.

I mean, I don't know the needs of your application, but remember that users 1) users can put anything they want in headers, and change them every request, totally circumventing this ratelimit, and 2) many users who are _not_ trying to circumvent the ratelimit will have the same user agent. The result is that User-Agent is a great way to punish good actors and a minor annoyance to bad ones. Combining it with IP just lowers the barrier to getting around the IP-only ratelimit (instead of needing to spoof your IP you can just change the UA).

----

Something like `X-Forwarded-For` (security review set aside for now) definitely does show the issue. This is my thinking:

If we had some way of enforcing "only use proxy-supplied/-validated headers", then I would say the right choice is, if it's not there: don't ratelimit (i.e. return `None` from `get_header` and if the value is `None`, return `False` early from `is_ratelimited`). That's better for testing (though we also have the global [`RATELIMIT_ENABLE`](http://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/settings.html) setting for that) and friendlier to internal-API use cases (though that can be addressed via [callable rates](http://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/rates.html)).

But we don't have any way of enforcing that.

Someone could ratelimit based on `User-Agent` or `X-Requested-With` or something else set by the UA itself, and the UA could simply not send it, and opt out of ratelimiting.

I don't want to give people a giant footgun that lets attackers simply say "please don't ratelimit me, thanks" because the ratelimit user didn't fully understand the docs. So I think @robinedwards's solution, `.get(header, '')` is correct, we just need to document that pretty thoroughly.

I think it's easiest if I just update the docs for [common keys](http://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/keys.html#common-keys) after this PR is merged.

----

It's probably also worth adding a section to the [security chapter](http://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/security.html) in the docs about ratelimiting with user-supplied headers. I'll plan on doing that, as well.

So I realized that this could in fact already be achieved by resetting the `render_server`'s `url` attribute dynamically. This is what I ended up doing:

```python
from subprocess import Popen, PIPE
from django.test.runner import DiscoverRunner
from django.conf import settings
from react.render_server import render_server


TEST_REACT_SERVER_HOST = getattr(settings, 'TEST_REACT_SERVER_HOST', '127.0.0.1')
TEST_REACT_SERVER_PORT = getattr(settings, 'TEST_REACT_SERVER_PORT', 9008)


class CustomTestRunner(DiscoverRunner):
    """
    Same as the default Django test runner, except it also runs our node
    server as a subprocess so we can render React components.
    """

    def setup_test_environment(self, **kwargs):
        # Start the test node server
        self.node_server = Popen(
            [
                'node',
                'react-server.js',
                '--host=%s' % TEST_REACT_SERVER_HOST,
                '--port=%s' % TEST_REACT_SERVER_PORT
            ],
            stdout=PIPE
        )
        # Wait until the server is ready before proceeding
        self.node_server.stdout.readline()
        # Point the renderer to our new test server
        render_server.url = 'http://%s:%s' % (
            TEST_REACT_SERVER_HOST, TEST_REACT_SERVER_PORT)
        super(CustomTestRunner, self).setup_test_environment(**kwargs)

    def teardown_test_environment(self, **kwargs):
        # Kill the node server
        self.node_server.terminate()
        super(CustomTestRunner, self).teardown_test_environment(**kwargs)
```

I'm not sure if that could be done differently in a way that wouldn't be backwards incompatible. Do you have any opinions? If not then I'm ok to just close this ticket and move forward with the solution above :)

The ideal solution sounds like a fair bit of work, so we're thinking of going for the second-best, ie, noticing when we've intercepted a POST request, and warning the user that they need to re-submit.

We're thinking of implementing it using a flag called, eg `sudo_post_intercepted` on the session, and we think we can do it all by overriding the `@sudo_required` decorator:

```python
def sudo_required(func):

    @wraps(func)
    def inner(request, *args, **kwargs):
        if not request.is_sudo():
            # we have intercepted a request that requires sudo
            # if POST, set sudo_post_intercepted flag
            # if GET, unset flag
            return redirect_to_sudo(request.get_full_path())

        # else:
        #  either the user already had sudo, and we are just passing straight through, 
        #  or the user just acquired sudo, and we are redirecting them
        #  back to where they were.
        #
        #  if we spot the was_post flag (which should only happen in redirect case),
        #  we should add a messages.warning "please resubmit your request"
        #  in either case, we un-set the sudo_post_intercepted flag.
        return func(request, *args, **kwargs)
    return inner
```

The reason I think the heating group would be very important to have is that to the best of my knowledge there's no useful way to control the newer heating hardware without it.

For the old devices there was a dumb HM-CC-VD per radiator and a smart HM-CC-TC per room. These did not overlap at all: You get status (battery, valve, ...) from the HM-CC-VD, and get/set status (current temperature, target temperature, ...) and configuration (heating program) from the HM-CC-TC.

The new HM-TC-IT-WM-W-EU and HM-CC-RT-DN are different: The HM-CC-RT-DN is fully functional on its own (so can be used mostly like a HM-CC-VD), but if you team multiple of them together and/or add a HM-TC-IT-WM-W-EU, you get a mess of settings and states: Each device has its own current and target temperature and its own schedule. The HM-TC-IT-WM-W-EU will send out updated target temperatures to all members of the team based on its internal schedule, and physically operating any of the team members will send updates to all other team members.

But: Operating the team members via RF requires you to send your commands to every member individually (and to receive status from all members and form your own opinion on what the current temperature setting is). It is possible (and very confusing) to set different target temperatures on a HM-TC-IT-WM-W-EU and HM-CC-RT-DN in a team. 

The virtual devices in the CCU take all the work out of this: The CCU already has an internal model that is updated from all the team members, and changing something on the virtual device makes the CCU send updates to all team members.

Hey @Sandy2904, right this is a problem I've heard before. For now, this is the model: 

```
      S(t) = exp(-(lambda*t)^rho),   lambda > 0, rho > 0,

    which implies the cumulative hazard rate is

      H(t) = (lambda*t)^rho,

    and the hazard rate is:

      h(t) = rho*lambda(lambda*t)^(rho-1)

```

I have an open issue to make this more clear in the documentation: https://github.com/CamDavidsonPilon/lifelines/issues/375. 

There is a nice interpretation of `rho` (here)[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weibull_distribution#Definition] (though they call it `k`)

This is a common problem. Let's work it up mathematically. 

Define S(t) := P(T > t), i.e. the survival function at time t

What you want is P(T > t | T > 30).

P(T > t | T > 30) = P(T > t and T > 30) / P(T > 30) = P(T > t) / P(T > 30) = S(t) / S(30)

So all you need to do is rescale the survival function by it's value at time 30.

Digging into this further, it appears that the problem is that (de)tox first creates an sdist, and then installs that sdist into its virtualenv before running the tests. Since `README.md` is not included in the sdist by default, when tox tries to run `setup.py` from the sdist, it can't find the `README.md` file to translate. The error from pypandoc is actually because there's a missing file, but it's being caught as a missing format.

The simplest solution is to add a `MANIFEST.in` file to your project with the following line:

```
include README.md
```

I'm not familiar with openID but with oauth2 you need to register an application to the third party authentication service (e.g. facebook) and use its client secret and id in order for the service to know who is asking for the data.

convertToken is more complex than this and there is more going on inside the django-oauth-toolkit package.
When we add this to NI-FGEN, we should keep the same signature for the public method and dispatch to the proper function internally if possible, rather than adding new public methods.

Digging around I found that the working approach is to remove all scheduler jobs before setting up (https://stackoverflow.com/a/32033454/235362) schedule. With flask-rq2 it would be:

```python

from . import tasks, rq
from datetime import datetime           

def setup():                                                                 
    """Set up the application schedule. Remove any already scheduled jobs."""
    scheduler = rq.get_scheduler()                                           
    for job in scheduler.get_jobs():                                         
        job.delete()                                                         

    tasks.my_task.schedule(datetime.utcnow(), interval=3600)           
```

Regarding the version number, you can set the `version` parameter for `odoorpc.ODOO` to prevent an extra RPC request on `/web/webclient/version_info`. The version number is required by `OdooRPC` to adapt its RPC queries depending on the server, if it is not supplied by the user it will try to guess it automatically.

Well, the problem is surely the package here https://github.com/OCA/odoorpc/tree/master/sphinx_ext .

I think I figured out a convenience wrapper version:

```python
def multigrad_dict(fun):
    "Takes gradients wrt all arguments simultaneously,"
    "returns a dict mapping 'argname' to 'gradval'"
    sig = funcsigs.signature(fun)

    def gradfun(*args, **kwargs):
        argdict = dict(sig.bind(*args, **kwargs).arguments)
        newfun = lambda dct: fun(**{key:dct[key] for key in argdict})
        return grad(newfun)(argdict)

    return gradfun
```

OK, thanks. Our support for nested data structures (here you've got arrays inside lists) isn't great, and you need to explicitly cast the lists to arrays in order for things to work. I've added the necessary casting and the following seems to work fine (although there appears to be some kind of numerical issue as it produces NaNs after a few iterations...
```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad
from autograd.misc.optimizers import adam
from autograd.scipy.misc import logsumexp
import autograd.numpy.random as npr


def init_random_params(theta, rs=npr.RandomState(0)):
    scale, layer_sizes = theta
    return [
        (
            rs.randn(insize, outsize) * scale,  # weight matrix
            rs.randn(outsize) * scale)  # bias vector
        for insize, outsize in zip(layer_sizes[:-1], layer_sizes[1:])
    ]


def g_nn(inputs, params, nonlinearity=np.tanh):
    for W, b in params:
        outputs = np.dot(inputs, W) + b
        inputs = nonlinearity(outputs)
    return outputs


def kl(m1, s1, m2, s2):
    return np.log(s2 / s1) + (s1**2 + (m1 - m2)**2) / (2 * s2**2) - 0.5


def wake_sleep(inputs, inputs_f, S, theta, alpha, grad_loss=None):
    nn_params = [1e-1, [1, 10, 10, 1]]
    z = [init_random_params(nn_params) for _ in range(S)]
    init_theta = theta

    def gaussian_pdf(x, mean, sigma):
        return 1. / (
            np.sqrt(2 * np.pi) * sigma) * np.exp(-0.5 *
                                                 (x - mean)**2 / sigma**2)

    def gaussian_logpdf(x, mean, sigma):
        return -np.log(2 * np.pi) - np.log(sigma) - 0.5 * (
            x - mean)**2 / sigma**2

    def bb_alpha_energy(theta, t):
        print(theta)
        m_a, s_a, sigma = theta
        a = np.random.normal(size=(S, S)) * s_a + m_a
        m_qgp = 1. / S * np.sum([g_nn(inputs, z_i) for z_i in z], axis=0)

        mc_q = []
        for a_k in a:
            m_k = []
            for z_s, a_s in zip(z, a_k):
                m_k.append((g_nn(inputs, z_s) - m_qgp) * a_s)
            m = (1. / np.sqrt(S - 1) * np.sum(np.array(m_k), axis=0))
            mc_q.append(gaussian_logpdf(inputs_f, m, sigma)**alpha)
        mc_q = 1. / S * np.log(np.sum(np.exp(np.array(mc_q)), axis=0))

        alpha_energy = 1. / alpha * np.sum(mc_q) - kl(m_a, s_a, 0., 1.)
        return alpha_energy

    if grad_loss is None:
        grad_loss = grad(bb_alpha_energy)
    opt_theta = adam(grad_loss, init_theta, step_size=0.01, num_iters=500)
    return opt_theta


if __name__ == '__main__':
    x = np.random.normal(size=(300, 1))
    f_x = np.cos(5 * x) / (np.abs(x) + 1)
    y = f_x + np.random.normal(scale=1e-1)

    m_a = np.random.normal(loc=0, scale=1e-2)
    s_a = 1e-1
    s = 1e-1
    init_theta = [m_a, s_a, s]

    S = 20
    alpha = 1.

    opt_theta = wake_sleep(x, f_x, S, init_theta, alpha)
    print(opt_theta)
```

If you are differentiating with respect to `a` and `b` directly, rather than them being some intermediates in the computation, you can also get them both at once (without redoing the forward pass or backward pass) using `multigrad`, which is in convenience_wrappers.py. Check out that file, since there is a lot of functionality in there.

You can also do the same thing manually by writing a function that takes an `(a, b)` tuple argument instead of separate arguments (since autograd will happily differentiate with respect to a tuple argument).

CFGAccurate recovers as much control-flow graph as possible by simulating the execution of programs. This means if the very first block of your program calls `exit()`, CFGAccurate will only contain a single block.

CFGFast works differently - the basic idea is recursive disassembly, without really simulating the execution.

> Could you please explain SimProcedure in more detail and how to patch this problem?

First, please refer to angr-doc and see what a SimProcedure is. Because of how CFGAccurate works, if there is a SimProcedure that is very important and simply skipping the call does not advance the execution of the program (i.e., the `__libc_start_main()` call, which creates three new exits whose addresses are passed as the first three arguments by the caller), you may want to implement it and make sure CFGAccurate picks it up when recovering the CFG.

> Does it mean that CFGAccurate will include all the node in CFGFast but also add more nodes?

"Accurate" is very different from "complete". So no, usually CFGAccurate contains way less nodes than CFGFast does.

One thing to note first is shape names are not used directly in response parsing. They really are only used as reference when traversing the model of an operation/service. It is the member name or location name needs to match the name of the XML tag.

For the root tag like CreateSnapshotResponse, the tag just provides a starting point to start parsing. This tag is never directly included in the dictionary returned by botocore. This means it is not important to have reference to the name of this root tag ahead of time in the model and therefore no guarantee needs to be made on the name of the shape for the output matching the name of the root tag.

Hope this helps. Let us know if you have any more questions.

In the probabilistic setting it makes sense : you want to maximize the conditional likelihood P(y_m,...,y_1 | x_n,...x_1) where x is the input sequence and y the output.

You decompose it as  
![codecogseqn 1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/10391785/21941551/4d544966-d997-11e6-8af4-a6250623688e.gif)

(Actually you look at the log likelihood)

Now at each timestep 
![codecogseqn 2](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/10391785/21941600/829f5318-d997-11e6-9427-01f10f6be219.gif) 
is actually the probability output by the RNN at time t with the true output for time 1 to t-1

Now this is only possible at training time. This is why decoding at test time is so important, ie beam search works better than greedily take the most probable word at each time step.

This can also be tackled at training time and I think that's what you're looking for specifically. One recent paper at NIPS did that http://papers.nips.cc/paper/6099-professor-forcing-a-new-algorithm-for-training-recurrent-networks.pdf but there are others.

Hopefully that answers your question

The issue I have with this PR has to do with the volume mount before we run `make server`. Volume mounts do not work on every machine (remote docker daemons e.g. boot2docker), which means this workflow will not work on all systems. Client/server autodocs won't be built without this volume mount, and because `docker build` only works in your current context, I fail to see a workaround unless we pulled the autodocs out (which won't be happening any time soon).

IMO best practices for volume mounts seem to be for saving container state on the host because of this limitation instead of mounting host resources into the container (exceptions to this would be mounting the docker socket, since that is guaranteed).

Other issues:

 - The `docker run -v` relative path arguments are very brittle. I'd rather reference an environment variable like $DEIS_ROOT to be more clear with the intention behind the volume.

If these two issues are addressed and everything else works fine, I'm happy with this as-is.

> The deisctl version I was using is 1.10.0 and this was a new cluster. Does the version get stored in the database?

That'll explain the issue. You need to make sure you're using version 1.10.0 *of the Deis code* to provision the hosts the cluster runs on - this sets an `/etc/deis-release` file which exports a platform version. This version is overwritten for upgrade installs by using `deisctl` to set `/deis/platform/version`, but when provisioning new hosts it's important to use the appropriate version of the code so your user-data prepares the filesystem in the expected way.

> I did a little exploratory coding for this, and I think the biggest problem here is going to be with new python 3 syntax. If the asyncio library isn't available in an python 2.7, that's not a problem because we can just conditionally not import it at runtime, and conditionally not define the retry_async version of the retry function. But if the code failed to compile because of new syntax e.g. yield from then it won't work and we are stuck. So the question is: is that new syntax required for the async path to work?

Uh, I forgot that `yield from` appeared only in Python 3.3. Yes, new syntax is required to use asyncio's coroutines (`yield from` and `asyncio.coroutine` are used in Python >=3.4, `await` and `async def` keywords may be used in Python >= 3.5).
We can move all async stuff to separate module, like `_backoff_async.py`, and import it only on Python >= 3.4.

Your workflow is possible, but if you have a single IAM role that needs to access multiple services, you need to do one of two things:

1. Make roles for each service, allow your instance role to assume these roles, then initialize a confidant client for each service, passing in the assumed role.
2. Use IAM policy to allow the instance to generate authentication tokens for any of the services. Initialize a confidant client for each service you want to fetch, adjusting the auth context for each intialization.

I'd recommend 2, especially since your IAM policy may already allow this for the key. Something to note here is that the auth context you use for the client needs to be scoped to the service you're fetching; so for instance, you probably want a context like:

```json
{'to': 'confidant-production', 'from': 'serviceA-production', 'user_type': 'service'}
```

I think here you also don't need to assume any role, since you're already using the confidant server's IAM role.

Ha. The virtualenv test suite is woefully limited (we don't even *have* CI on Windows). That's an acknowledged issue, but no-one has ever had the time to address it. It's one of the major reasons it's so hard to get fixes in - the huge diversity of systems that virtualenv is used on, coupled with the fact that virtualenv is basically a collection of hacks around quirks in those systems, and the fact that we have no tests to verify that we haven't broken stuff, leaves every PR at risk of breaking something that someone relies on. That's why (for example) I won't touch PRs for virtualenv on Unix, as I don't use Unix myself.

I reproduced this issue on debian stable (I'm trying to debian package it).
The 0.6.1 version is the actual version packaged from debian (python3-cryptography) on the current stable (jessie).

On a debian-stable:
```
$ lsb_release -a
Distributor ID: Debian
Description:    Debian GNU/Linux 8.5 (jessie)
Release:        8.5
Codename:       jessie
$ apt-cache show python3-cryptography
...
Package: python3-cryptography
Source: python-cryptography
Version: 0.6.1-1
```

I believe this problem won't occurred from cryptography >= 1.1.
Since it's from that moment on that `cryptography.hazmat.primitives.keywrap` module appears.
(I've looked at the git-history of the debian package which imports upstream tarballs, and that module appears at that version).

Note that on debian-testing, since this package is more advanced, there won't be issue about it:
```
$ lsb_release -a
No LSB modules are available.
Distributor ID: Debian
Description:    Debian GNU/Linux testing (stretch)
Release:        testing
Codename:       stretch
$ apt-cache show python3-cryptography
Package: python3-cryptography
Source: python-cryptography
Version: 1.5-1
```

At last, there may be not much people bothered by this since they may use pip/pip3 to install their package.

So, i guess your workaround would be to use pip3 yourself for now.
Or, if you already do, try and upgrade your cryptography version, i believe it's:

```
sudo pip3 install cryptography --upgrade
```

I'll do some thinking on how to have components specify grids they want. For now, I think the following works pretty cleanly from an API standpoint.

- I've written `get_grid` to take in an option of `regular` or `gaussian` for the latitudinal grid type. The idea is that if you pass `gaussian`, it should make a grid that works with GFS.
- When GFS is called the first time, it should initialize its grid as it currently does using the number of points in the latitudinal and longitudinal directions. It should then check whether its lat/lon grids are within some epsilon of the lat/lon grids passed in the state, and error if they are not.

Moving forward, we can think about how to have components specify with properties that they want a certain type of grid and have the initializer give that grid. I'd like it to be something where the components only have properties similar to their properties dictionary, and the code to actually create the grid lives in the initialization library (with plans to later have a way to register this type of initializer with Sympl).

For now the only component that has a requirement on the grid is GFS, and I have `get_grid` return a Gaussian grid by default, so this should be clean and safe.

Some comments: you're focusing on what kinto does for the users at first, stating what they want, but this might or might not be the case, depdending who your users are.

I proposed a few changes (in the pad and here):

```rst

We believe data belongs to the users, and not to the application authors. When writing an application, data should be made available from any device, connected or offline, and everything should be in sync.

Rather than spending a non-trivial amount of time and expertise on implementing all that (and then maintaining it !), you could use Kinto, that does all that for you:

    - Expose your data over an HTTP interface, unlike a database like PostreSQL
    - Use simple HTTP queries, instead of SQL
    - Use Kinto.js to easily implement offline first clients
    - Choose the database you want from those that Kinto supports, and use a unified API to access its data
    - Administrate your data using the handy admin UI
    - Easily set up live push notifications for live synchronisation
    - Make it possible to  share data between users (using fine-grained permissions)
    - Take advantage of schema validation if you need it
    - Use tools and libraries like Kinto.js that implements encryption so you don't have to
```

About the wording: we try in general in the documentation to avoid using "you".

The path being complained about is:

    /home/willi/anaconda/pkgs/cbmpy-0.7.11-py27_1/Lib/site-packages/cbmpy-0.7.11-py2.7.egg-info

After conda downloads and unpackages the file, you'll find it in your cache at 

    /home/willi/anaconda/pkgs/cbmpy-0.7.11-py27_1/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cbmpy-0.7.11-py2.7.egg-info

The reason conda thinks the file is at

    Lib/site-packages/cbmpy-0.7.11-py2.7.egg-info

instead of 

    lib/python2.7/site-packages/cbmpy-0.7.11-py2.7.egg-info

is because of what's listed in the `info/paths.json` file.  Note that this *doesn't* match what's in the `info/files` file, which is the older version of the package manifest.

> @groutr: If I understand features correctly, only the package at the bottom of the stack needs track features.

In practice it seems to be more complicated than that. I can reproduce an "unsatisfiable dependencies" error with the following minimal example:
```yaml
# track_feat/meta.yaml
package:
  name:  track_feat
  version: "1.0"

build:
  features:
    - feat
  track_features:
    - feat
```
```yaml
# feat/meta.yaml
package:
  name:  feat
  version: "1.0"

build:
  features:
    - feat

requirements:
  build:
    - track_feat
  run:
    - track_feat
```
Then on the command line:
```sh
>> conda build  track_feat     # works
>> conda build  feat           # works
>> conda create --use-local -n feat  feat  # fails: 'feat' has unsatisfiable dependencies
```
According to my understanding of the documentation, this is supposed to work. 
There are two possibilities to fix the example:
1. Add a `track_features: feat` directive to `feat/meta.yaml`.
2. Install `track_feat` separately:
```sh
>> conda create --use-local -n feat  track_feat
>> activate feat
>> conda install --use-local feat
```

Likely because they were first installed with conda, but then installed again with pip.  Conda inspects the installed packages in an environment via the contents of the environment's `conda-meta` directory.  Other package managers (like pip, gem, npm, etc) can clobber an environment in several ways.

We look for pip installed packages by inspecting the environment's python `site-packages` directory and looking for `.egg-info` and `.dist-info` directories to pull metadata out of (maybe a few more sources also).  Those are the python packages that have been installed NOT as conda packages.  This should include btw installing via `python setup.py install`.

 Someday in the future we'll probably add a `conda doctor` or `conda integrity-check` or something for an environment.  That would be able to report on files that have been added to the environment or altered without conda knowing about it.  We're actually right now adding the information to conda-build packages to be able to do that (https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/1501).  But the actual implementation of that feature within conda is lower priority than many other features and fixes right now.

We discussed a lot about this matter when we were doing the barceloneta theme. So we ended doing our own structured less infraestructure that does not depend on bootstrap and provides a compatibility layer. Bootstrap is not only a matter of css is also js, and in our case we are using patterns so it does not make sense to use only half of bootstrap.

If we wanna upgrade barceloneta less files to be compatible with a new bootstrap you just need to check the correspondent less file on barceloneta theme folder and change it to the one you want to upgrade (that provides the option to change the grid system to use any other you want just overwriting the gris.plone.less)

I know this aproach has its cons ( its not as simple to put a bootstrap theme on top of barceloneta theme ) but its not a big work to adapt it. We are used to use comercial bootstrap themes on the markup.

As my mine pros are depending on bootstrap css part is not really usefull, that ties our hands, creates a three layer system ( bootstrap - barceloneta - own theme ) that may end with lots of conflics. I love that we don't depend on bootstrap but we have the same compatibility. At the end is good for integrators that they know that the main css is not going to change as often as bootstrap is doing. Finally it does not make sense that we use modal/dropdown... css from bootstrap ( most of the css on bootstrap is realeted to js components ) as we have patterns...

The way this is written could result in duplicate work if threads race to read the same nodes. If I understand correctly, the nodes are files that make up the feed and reading some nodes can cause other nodes to be read as well. Some of those nodes might be in the set originally requested, and so will be requested more than once, possibly by different threads.

The best way to solve this is probably the implement concurrency on the feed object itself. The use of `lru_cache` there is probably not safe right now because it will leak memory. The `lru_cache` decorator doesn't handle methods any special way, so a single instance of it will be shared between all instances and caching on `(self, filename)` such that the cache keeps instances alive indefinitely.

I spent a bit more time reading up on SSH multiplexing. (I was prompted by some benign multiplexing errors, interestingly enough.) I happily rediscovered the `-o` option provided by the client, and tested them with this script:

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash

time for i in {1..10}; do
    ssh example.com hostname
done

time for i in {1..10}; do
    ssh \
        -o 'ControlMaster=auto' \
        -o 'ControlPersist=10s' \
        -o 'ControlPath=~/.ssh/controlmasters/%C' \
        example.com hostname
done
```

It works well. Given this, we could default to inserting these options into SSH commands:

    -o ControlMaster=auto -o ControlPersist=5m -o ControlPath=$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/pulp_smash/controlmasters/%C

How can we control these options? (I think it's a Bad Idea to hard-code any options in.)  One option is to support a `PULP_SMASH_SSH_ARGS` environment variable. Another option is to expand the configuration file. In either case, we should use a default argument only if the user has not provided their own.

Using default arguments in this manner is with precedent. See Ansible's [OpenSSH Specific Settings](http://docs.ansible.com/ansible/intro_configuration.html#openssh-specific-settings) documentation as an analogue.

This solution will require some environment inspection and manipulation. Specifically, we will need to inspect whether `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` is set and provide a default value if unset. (Defaults for defaults!) It'll also require that we create the directory in which sockets go. @marcoabreu The implementation of `shuffle` already uses parallelism. On GPU, it utilizes parallel sorting algorithm and the memory swap is also parallelized. On CPU it uses GNU's parallel implementation of `std::random_shuffle` if the array is 1D. Only for higher dimensional arrays in CPU, the implementation is sequential. The optimization in this case is not trivial due to the diversity of data shape and hardware. I have tested a naive parallelization of the memory copy for the case. You can see the result in this [comment](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/10048#discussion_r173857649). You have asked the test but I forgot mentioning you at the comment :)

Let's clarify a bit, because I'm not sure we're all in the same page here.

The assumption is that we will have two raw (Perceval) indexes: 

* `github`, the current GitHub index, from the GitHub issues API.
* `githubpr`, the new GitHun PR index, from the GutHub Pull Requests API.

Now, with those, we would be producing a *new* enriched index, `githubpr`, with all the data needed to deal with PRs. That data would come from the two previous raw indexes, and yes, it may contain duplicated data with the current `github` enriched index.

I think at some point we would do the same with a new `githubissues` index, which will carry data only on issues, deprecating the current `github` enriched index.

[Elaborating on how we should use Fire to write this.]

Ideally we could do something like this:

```
import fire

def launch_cli(string, *args):
  ...  # This is where we use imp to get the module.
  fire.Fire(module, args=args)

if __name__ == '__main__':
  fire.Fire(launch_cli)
```

This lets us avoid having to deal with sys.argv at all.

However there might be some issues w/ this approach:
- which call to Fire gets the flags? (A: by default, the first call to Fire would get the flags, and only if there's additional flags and an additional '--' would the inner call to Fire get those flags. that's not ideal and we'd have to find a way around this).
- we may need a workaround to prevent the arguments from being parsed prematurely. (decorators.py has such a workaround)

What do you think about the following?

If:
- an argument starts with '--'  (e.g. `--arg`)
- AND the argument is not surrounded in quotes (in practice this means being double surrounded in quotes :/) e.g. (`'"--arg"'`)
- AND the argument is not prefixed with an equal sign (e.g. `--someflag=--arg`)

Then:
- We shouldn't treat the arg as a string at all, since the user probably meant for the arg to be a flag, not a value.

`py::array` will automatically copy your data if you don't give it a `base` argument in the constructor (though maybe that's indeed not very well documented).

If you don't want to copy, one solution would be to move the `std::vector` into a `py::capsule` and use that capsule as the `base` for a new `py::array`, then just continue using the `v.data()` of that moved vector to construct. This is how I envision the API working:
> examples.py
```python
example_context = CartoContext(...)

def load_taxi():
    return example_context.read('taxi_dataset')

def load_mexico_city():
    return example_context.read('mexico_city_metro')

Usage in a script would be like:
```python
from cartoframes import examples

# get dataframe of taxi data
taxi_df = examples.load_taxi()

Short answer: yes.
But it is a bit more complicated. What I currently do is to package the latest version of photutils for the next versions of Debian (and also implicitly Ubuntu). This version gets fixed when the distribution goes into a freeze (~1,5 months for Ubuntu, ~6 months for Debian). After that, the version that is directly in the distribution remains unchanged. Only bug fixes are possible then (no new versions).
To get a new version running on an older Debian/Ubuntu version, one needs to do a "backport", which may include a backport of dependent packages (f.e. when the new version of photcal requires a newer version of astropy). I don't have the manpower to do backporting myself (I do the packaging in my spare time).
So, if you see a need here, and if you want to help us, please volunteer to backport. I gladly offer any help to get it done. If nobody volunteers, I am afraid that you have to live with the version that was there when your Ubuntu distribution was released. My email is olebole@debian.org -- just contact me.

I think we should think about what we do here carefully. Silently excluding large segments of the Certbot user base from TLS security updates without very loudly warning them about it first and giving them an alternative is probably a bad idea. Despite our frustration with it, I think this means we can't exclude `certbot-auto` as it is still the largest portion of Certbot users. Users of other packaging methods we may use such as `pyinstaller`  or distros we either don't communicate with or won't make this change (properly) will also be left out of security updates with this approach. Is this what we want?

Having distros solve this problem is the conventional approach, however, I personally expect getting everyone to do this will be more work than just solving the problem ourselves with a consistent approach across systems that supports whatever alternative packaging method such `certbot-auto`, `pip`, `pyinstaller`, `docker`, etc.

After thinking about this over the weekend, I'm personally in favor of adding a simple version of what distros do with modified configuration files ourselves. This could look something like maintaining a list of files/hashes of previous versions of the file, overwriting the file with the new version if it's in the list and creating a `options-ssl-nginx.conf.new` with a warning otherwise. This warning/behavior could be turned off with something like a `--no-tls-updates` flag. There's a couple of additional minor details to work out here, but I think it'd be fairly simple to implement.

@jpluimers this is not currently explained anywhere, but I believe we have an issue somewhere to explain this in the developer guide. We currently try to hide the fact that lineages exist at all from Certbot users, instead using the "renewal" fiction.

A lineage is a series of certificates that have replaced each other, currently either for the same set of domain names or for an increasing set of domain names when using the `--expand` flag. Each lineage has an associated config file under the `renewal` directory, and its name is the name of the config file. We don't ever actually renew certificates (this isn't a thing), we just get a new one and point the symlink under `live` to the new cert.

I'm going to break this into a few different pieces:

### Parsing Output

I just ran a quick test via `--dry-run`. It looks like `all renewals succeeded` should be present in [the success output](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/v0.20.0/certbot/renewal.py#L343). Something like this will fail when the renewal doesn't succeed and completely hide output:

```bash
$ (certbot renew | grep "all renewals succeeded") >/dev/null 2>&1; echo $?
1
$ (certbot renew --dry-run | grep "all renewals succeeded") >/dev/null 2>&1; echo $?
0
```
The tailing `echo` is just there to explicitly display the status.

### Masking Output

You can't easily hide some of `STDOUT` and show the rest. It looks like [hooks are piped directly to `STDOUT`](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/v0.20.0/certbot/hooks.py#L233), so `--quiet` is going to mute hooks as well. But, since hooks can be full scripts, you can pipe their output to another file.

There might be a way to mask things with a `pre_hook` and restore them with a `post_hook`. I've tried a couple of different things with no luck. I ran out of easy ideas there.

### Brittle Solution

Assuming `certbot` is `renew`ing on a schedule, you could also schedule a load-balancer restart (or at least parse a shared file to possibly trigger a restart) a little bit after the `renew` command. I'd recommend not doing that, though, because you have to assume way too much about your environment working as intended all the time.

### Communicating Between Containers

No matter what, you're going to have to pass information between containers somehow.

* You can [share a file](https://superuser.com/questions/181517/how-to-execute-a-command-whenever-a-file-changes) over a volume.
* You could log to a system channel on the host and trigger actions there (e.g. `systemd` with a tool like [`journald-triggerd`](https://jjacky.com/journal-triggerd/)).
* You can connect a port and [listen for commands](https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/139315)
* You can [`POST` a message via Python 2](https://docs.python.org/2/library/cgihttpserver.html) or [Python 3](https://docs.python.org/3/library/http.server.html?highlight=http%20server#http.server.CGIHTTPRequestHandler) (at least one box has to have Python because of Certbot)
* You can [CURL a request](https://gist.github.com/joyrexus/85bf6b02979d8a7b0308) from Certbot to the load balancer and use the load balancer's server to execute a script (e.g. [Nginx](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/22891148/nginx-how-to-run-a-shell-script-on-every-request))

With Compose, you should be able to directly connect a pair of ports at launch. If one machine is just sending, say, `1`, to the other, there's not a whole lot to worry about.

At this point, I think a really good question is how did you hope to trigger a load balancer restart? Assuming you could get `certbot` to do whatever you wanted, how were you planning to handle it?

I want to echo the issue that @parente is adding to this PR and maybe the hardest to fix; the loading of dependency libraries and the right timing to render/execute cell output. 

As we've been working in declarativewidgets on a way to change how the user initializes the extension on a particular notebook, we've been struggling a lot with the 'chicken or the egg' problem. We've tried many things, and along the way, it was surprising to find out that on a page refresh, cell output is rendered before extensions are fully loaded. I guess the limitation is understandable after you think about the implications, but at least form me, that was an expectation.

Anyway, I think that to fully understand this issue, we need to think about different scenarios on the client side and the timing of execution as it relates to kernel code and client side extension/library. Here are some of the ones that I can think of.

1. User creates a new notebook and executes a cell that requires some client side code. (when is that cell really done)
2. User visits an existing notebook that is cleared of output but performs a Run all (inter-cell dependencies)
3. User saves a notebook with output and refreshes the browser. (rendering of cell output in relation to dependencies being loaded)
4. User restarts the kernel and re-run cells (client side code is already loaded, should it be reinit)

For all the above we need to answer:
 * when can cells be executed?
 * when can cell output be rendered?
 * when is the cell output done so that the next cell can execute

- Tax rates are read from cache and, if cache fails, read from DB.

In my case, I'm using Bleach to sanitize the HTML (after Markdown parsing) from user posts/comments. It's pretty common for people to write a comment using angle brackets around a "marker" like:

> Please write your responses in this format: "Favorite movie: <name>", thanks.

html5lib seems to be "fixing" these, even if they're not valid HTML tags. The result from running a comment like that through `clean()` is:

> Please write your responses in this format: "Favorite movie: <name>", thanks.</name>

It's even worse if they include spaces, like `<name of movie>`:

> Please write your responses in this format: "Favorite movie: <name movie="" of="">", thanks.</name>

Do you think it's feasible at all to be able to disable this behavior? I could try to help with it, if needed.

Hi @lukejanicke. You've run into a common point of confusion when using multiple versions of Python. Let's see if I can give you a bit more insight.

Yes, there can be two or more kernel files (Python3, Python2, R, Julia, etc.). For example, on my system, I currently have two kernels installed (Python3 and Python2):
```
$ jupyter kernelspec list
Available kernels:
  python3    /anaconda3/share/jupyter/kernels/python3
  python2    /usr/local/share/jupyter/kernels/python2
```

### Finding your python versions

Unrelated to the kernels, I can find where I have Python installed by using the `which` command:
```
carol at cw-pro in ~
$ which python3
/anaconda3/bin/python3

carol at cw-pro in ~
$ which python2
/usr/local/bin/python2

carol at cw-pro in ~
$ which python
/anaconda3/bin/python
```
My system has the `/anaconda3/bin/python` symlinked (i.e. aliased) to `/anaconda3/bin/python3`.

### Understanding the nteract recommendation

The pattern that nteract recommends is a one that follows the 
Looking at the recommendation that nteract gives:
```
python -m pip install ipykernel
python -m ipykernel install --user
```
In the case of my system, the command `python -m pip install ipykernel` would be the same as if I typed `/anaconda3/bin/python -m pip install ipykernel`. When the command executes python, it aliases to `/anaconda3/bin/python3`. By using `python -m pip`, you are essentially telling the system to use the version of pip installed with the particular version of Python that `which python` returns.

For example, `python2 -m pip install ipykernel` would use `/usr/local/bin/python2` to execute pip to install the kernel.

So to recap up to this point, there are different Python versions on my computer. I can find them with the `which` command. `python -m`, `python2 -m` and `python3 -m` are shortcuts to the different Pythons that are displayed when entering the `which` command.

Hope this helps a bit.
