@suntao2012 The use of bendpoints is a little bit tricky with gmf notation as they put some manual logic in the original metamodel code. 
If you followed the small guide I wrote, you should have a `BendPoint` class with a dedicated `from_string` and `to_string` methods. For the example her, I put it in the code so I can access it using `notation.BenPoint`. You will have to adapt the snippet.

```python
bendpoints = notation.RelativeBendpoints()
bendpoints.points = [
    notation.BendPoint(590, 341, 556, 319),
    notation.BendPoint(617, 357, 583, 335)
]
```
For the explanation, each bendpoint is gathered in a `RelativeBenpoints` object, and stored in the `points` attribute in a list.

Then, you can add the `bendpoints` to your `edge_node_root`:

```python
edge_node_root.bendpoints = bendpoints
```

For setting the `source` and `target`, this is the same idea than for the `benpoints`, you can simply do:

```python
edge_node_root.source = ...  # your source object
edge_node_root.targe = ...  # your target object
```

Spot on. It appears [this line](https://github.com/pytroll/python-geotiepoints/blob/0357eaa73e5f5843154f442ddbb4df693d280097/geotiepoints/interpolator.py#L255) is the trouble maker:

            self.new_data[num] = self.new_data[num].reshape(xpoints.shape).T

Changing it to:
```
            self.new_data[num] = self.new_data[num].reshape(xpoints.shape).T.copy(order='C')
```

Currently I'm thinking that the scope of the dynamic configuration would be limited to PostgreSQL parameters, `postgresql.conf`, `pg_hba.conf` and `pg_ident.conf`.

We are looking to do something to serve 2 different purposes:

1. change global configuration of all current (and future) Patroni members
2. change the configuration of a single current node

Point 2 can - and should IMHO - be addressed by using `ALTER SYSTEM`.
Point 1 cannot be addressed fully by `ALTER SYSTEM`, a new replica may be created from an older backup which may not have the recent `ALTER SYSTEM` registered in `postgresql.auto.conf`. A workaround could be to query the master's current settings.

We also would like to be able to reconfigure `pg_hba.conf` and if needed, `pg_ident.conf`, as there may be a valid reason to distinguish between allowed connections to 1 member and to other members.

Restarting PostgreSQL can currently already be done by Patroni, using the restart endpoint: https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/patroni/api.py#L103
This can restart PostgreSQL without restarting Patroni.

Hi @bradnicholson, thank you for testing a new feature.
There are definitely some issues with hba_file and custom bootstrap, but it's not really clear what is happening with your setup. Some Patroni logs will definitely help to clarify the situation.

How this feature suppose to work:

1. Patroni calls custom_bootstrap script
2. Patroni creates a new pg_hba.conf  with trust access in the data_dir and starts postgres with hba_file = ${postgresql.data_dir}/pg_hba.conf (this is intentional). 
3. When postgres will reach `not pg_is_ in_recovery()` state, Patroni will connect and CREATE/ALTER superuser and replication users with desired passwords (this is the reason why we opened trust access)
4. Patroni will revert changes to desired (custom hb_file) and do `pg_ctl reload`

What can be wrong: you run Patroni with $USER != "postgres". initdb will create not superuser=postgres in the cluster, but superuser=$USER and Patroni will not be able to connect (step 3), because it tries to connect as "postgres" database user. 
In this case you will see in Patroni logs lots of following lines:
```
FATAL:  role "postgres" does not exist
localhost:5432 - accepting connections
FATAL:  role "postgres" does not exist
localhost:5432 - accepting connections
2017-07-20 11:42:07,670 INFO: establishing a new patroni connection to the postgres cluster
FATAL:  role "postgres" does not exist
2017-07-20 11:42:07,902 INFO: establishing a new patroni connection to the postgres cluster
FATAL:  role "postgres" does not exist
FATAL:  role "postgres" does not exist
```

This can be solved by specifying `--username=postgres` argument to `initdb`, but unfortunately it doesn't solve some other problem and step 4 will fail:
```
2017-07-20 11:46:07,815 INFO: running post_bootstrap
LOG:  received SIGHUP, reloading configuration files
server signaled
LOG:  parameter "data_directory" cannot be changed without restarting the server
LOG:  parameter "hba_file" cannot be changed without restarting the server
LOG:  parameter "ident_file" cannot be changed without restarting the server
LOG:  configuration file "/home/akukushkin/patroni/data/postgresql0/postgresql.conf" contains errors; unaffected changes were applied
LOG:  could not open configuration file "/home/akukushkin/patroni/data/postgresql0/pg_hba.conf": No such file or directory
WARNING:  pg_hba.conf not reloaded
```

Obviously we need to restart postgres to change location of `pg_hba.conf` :(
Will prepare a fix for that.

Is using the API to construct a tactic really an alternative? Can such a solver be combined with an incremental solver, easily?

Our original idea (which is, admittedly, based on an "uneducated guess") was to let the incremental solver try to solve the problem first (but only for a short amount of time) and if it fails to produce an answer, to automatically resort to a non-incremental solver. In this way, we hoped to solve simple instances incrementally whereas leaving the hard instances to a more powerful solver. We have used this in a predicate abstraction framework, where predicates are represented by activation literals. So our expectation was that an incremental solver has the chance to learn facts about predicate formulas that it can re-use in subsequent queries that involve the same predicates.

I hope to be able to produce a benchmark to see if the change has any impact on our verifier's performance.

wrote:

> When using aiobotocore with dynamodb, requests fail on crc32 checksum
> computation as soon as the response data reaches ~5KB.
>
> It seems that this is due to aws sending data back in gzip compressed
> format, aiohttp automatically uncompressing it and crc32 checksum being
> computed on the uncompressed data bytes.
> While crc32 has to be computed on compressed data in order to pass.
>
> The same requests work as expected when using botocore, this commit forces
> aws not to use gzip compression.
>
> I created this fix to show the problem and to temporarily patch the issue,
> but the right way would be configuring aiohttp not to uncompress the
> response and leave that up to botocore, so we can take advantage of the
> gzip compression when data >= ~5KB.

It's worth noting that the daemon model of watchman is a big part of what makes watchman great. It might make the onboarding user experience a bit better as a node extension but as a daemon you get instant results when querying the watched source tree, and with the `watch-project` command we can consolidate multiple watches if they overlap which makes it perform better.

If watchman can solve this reliably for every platform (I hear windows support is in the works). Then *all* developers should have it installed so we can get this thing behind us already and get on with our work. If your project introduces your developers to watchman they'll install it once and any other tool can benefit from the same install. For example, if you used react-native, you should be able to use webpack with the same watchman install.

The `amqp` plugin uses `kombu` as the driver precisely so that you can swap the backend for whatever you like (as long as it's supported by kombu, of course). And if you look closely at the `setttings.py` (https://github.com/guardian/alerta/blob/master/alerta/settings.py#L44) you'll actually see that by default `alertad` doesn't use RabbitMQ it uses MongoDB as the AMQP transport.

If you want to use AMQP plugin I suggest you switch to RabbitMQ or Redis but you'll need to install these as additional components.

Note that you only need to enable the `amqp` plugin if you are making use of the email or pagerduty integrations available [here](https://github.com/alerta/alerta-contrib/tree/master/integrations/amqp) or have written an AMQP integration of your own. However, if you were integrating pagerduty I would suggest using the [pagerduty plugin](https://github.com/alerta/alerta-contrib/tree/master/plugins/pagerduty) as it's easier to implement.

The AMQP integrations used to be the main was of integrating third-party apps however plugins are now the preferred method so I think I will remove the `amqp` plugin from the list of default plugins from now on.

Hi @gracaninja, thanks so much for this report! It's actually a bit confusing, but `Mixpanel.people_set`—in fact, all of the `people_*` methods—accepts two arguments. The first contains the data to set on the people profile, and the second one (`meta`) manages metadata about the operation itself. Our [HTTP API Reference](https://mixpanel.com/help/reference/http#people-analytics-updates) lists properties that need to be set in the `meta` dict. It looks like the relevant ones in your case are `$ip` and `$ignore_time`, so you'll want to make a call like:

```python
connection.people_set(user.username, people_dict, meta={
    '$ip': convert_datetime_to_string(user.current_ip),
    '$ignore_time': True,
})
```

This definitely ought to be in the `mixpanel-python` docs! I'll update the title of this issue to reflect that.

miniterm, by default filters out escape sequences and micropython makes use of a number of them.

if you are using a Posix system, you usually have a terminal (e.g. "VT100" or better) that is capable to decode those escapes. it is an option to configure miniterm to pass through escape codes. However, that usually does not work on Windows where `ansi.sys` would add support but no one load it.

Specifically for micropython you may be interested in https://github.com/zsquareplusc/mpy-repl-tool where the `-i` switch starts a patched miniterm. at least under Linux it works well and with the help of `colorma` it works partially under Windows too.

@bagder This is specific to kerberos- basically, do we attempt to decrypt the final WWW-Authenticate response header with our session key to ensure that it came from the server we think we're communicating with, vs just ignoring it and saying, "welp, we got a 200 and a response, good to go!"- REQUIRED says we always validate and error if missing, OPTIONAL says validate if present (but ignore if missing), and DISABLED always ignores. I'm not sure how much value that final check really adds, though- I haven't thought through all the different attack mechanisms.

tl;dr this is correct behaviour.

Slightly longer version: `"\r"` is a carriage return symbol, which when encountered by the stream, cases the cursor to go to the beginning of line. Your input has a carriage return in the middle, therefore it is rendered as

```
ipcs -s | grep nobody |awk '{print$2}'|xargs -n1 ipcrm sem ;ps aux|grep -P 'http
```

then the screen goes to the new line and renders everything before `"\r"`

```
d|fcgi'|grep -v grep|awk '{print$2
                                 ^ 
                               cursor
```

on `"\r"` the cursor moves to the beginning of line

```
d|fcgi'|grep -v grep|awk '{print$2
^
cursor
```

and continues rendering remaining input from there

```
}'|xargs kill -9;/etc/init.d/httpd startssl
```

The resulting state is exactly what you're observing.

We plan to minimize the amount of data that needs to be downloaded by making it work on blocks. Going for the same overhead target as you do :)

In case of encryption, we need some kind of header, so we'll get it once and keep it in memory until you close the file handle. In case of compression, it is tricky, but if a file-like object is used, we can do much better than with a crude approach.

Large files should be handled completely transparently.

Therefore if we'd keep B2 API layer very thin, someone would have to create another library that would wrap it into something usable. That does not make much sense. It is better if we do it once in the Python B2 API layer and everyone just uses it in a civilized way. One of good examples of such improvement is retrying uploads when something goes wrong.

@juanchopanza I just learned about this issue and what you describe is not considered an error in the morphology. According to the specs (http://research.mssm.edu/cnic/swc.html) there is no restriction about the ordering. Besides there was a long discussion a couple of months ago (about h5 format) and the outcome was that we should not assume any ordering.

Coming back to the example you describe, the soma points consist a single section. That means we cannot have a bifurcation within the soma. A tree starting from any point of the soma that is not the last one is not only allowed but is actually what we have in the majority of the swc data. 

So the following is correct:
```
1 1 x y z d -1
2 3 x y z d 1
3 3 x y z d 2
4 1 x y z d 1
```
and it is just a matter of ordering of the points.

But this is not correct:
```
1 1 x y z d -1
2 1 x y z d 1
3 3 x y z d 2
4 1 x y z d 1
```
because it assumes there is a bifurcation within the soma. 

From my understanding we have the first case, so the morphology is correct and just the ordering of the points is problematic. Please let me know if I misinterpreted something from the previous discussion.

This is happening because the `requests` library will accept a URL with unicode in it and the resulting `response.url` will be in escaped unicode, making it hard to compare the originally passed URL to the `repsonse.url`.

For example

    >>> import requests
    >>> r = requests.get('https://hi.wikipedia.org/wiki/दिलवाले_दुल्हनिया_ले_जाएंगे')

As for `mktemp`, the `--tmpdir` option is not available under OS X. There are only two possibilities: we can provide an absolute path as a template, or we can use the -t option to provide a prefix and let the command generate the template using the default temporary directory. Since the original source code used `mktemp` on its own, I assumed the intent was to let the command choose the temporary directory, which on OS X can only be done through the -t option. But, of course, as you suggested, `mktemp "${TMPDIR:-/tmp}/powerline.XXXXXXXX"` would indeed work just fine on all systems.

``````````
class CommandExecutionMixin(object):

    class InvalidCommand(F5SDKError):
        """Raise this if command argument supplied is invalid."""
        pass

    class UnsupportedMethod(F5SDKError):
        pass

    def create(self, **kwargs):
        '''Create is not supported for command execution

        :raises: UnsupportedOperation
        '''
        raise self.UnsupportedMethod(
            "%s does not support the create method" % self.__class__.__name__
        )

    def delete(self, **kwargs):
        '''Delete is not supported for command execution

        :raises: UnsupportedOperation
        '''
        raise self.UnsupportedMethod(
            "%s does not support the delete method" % self.__class__.__name__
        )

    def load(self, **kwargs):
        '''Load is not supported for command execution

                :raises: UnsupportedOperation
        '''
        raise self.UnsupportedMethod(
            "%s does not support the load method" % self.__class__.__name__
        )


    def exec_cmd(self, command, **kwargs):
        '''Create a new method as command has specific requirements.

        Command commands do not have direct mapping to an HTTP method
        so usage of POST and an absolute URI is required. There is
        a handful of the TMSH global commands supported, so this method
        requires them as a parameter.

        :raises: InvalidCommand
        '''

        cmds = ['cp', 'generate', 'install', 'load', 'mv', 'publish',
                'reboot', 'restart', 'reset-stats', 'run', 'save',
                'send-mail', 'start', 'stop']
        if command not in cmds:
            error_message = "The command value {0} does not exist" \
                "Valid commands are {1}".format(command, cmds)
            raise InvalidCommand(error_message)

        kwargs['command'] = command
        requests_params = self._handle_requests_params(kwargs)

        # We won't always need a name parameter, sometimes commands
        # can be run with just command:reboot.
        self._meta_data['required_creation_parameters'].update()

        session = self._meta_data['bigip']._meta_data['icr_session']
        # Invoke the REST operation on the device.
        response = session.post(
            self._meta_data['uri'], json=kwargs, **requests_params)

        # Post-process the response
        self._local_update(response.json())

        if self.kind != self._meta_data['required_json_kind']:
            error_message = "For instances of type '%r' the corresponding" \
                            " kind must be '%r' but creation returned JSON with kind: %r" \
                            % (self.__class__.__name__,
                               self._meta_data['required_json_kind'],
                               self.kind)
            raise KindTypeMismatch(error_message)

        return self

``````````

I'll add my two cent here.

I don't think it is good behavior for a python package to search for an automatically use a python version that is not the default system version. That is the kind of "magic" which causes sleepless nights when debugging.

Also, relying on heuristics and default install locations to check which versions are available is prone to break, given how many flavors of linux exits.

One should simply use `sys.version_info` to check for the required python version and issue an informative error message if the version is not appropriate.
The error message can then instruct the user how he can configure ganga to use a non-default python version.

http://stackoverflow.com/a/446136/1375015

You make a good point about the time. I think it is because ganga looks up the list of locations for the DiracFile and then gets the accessURL for a specified location picked from the list. Both of these things take a little time.

We can certainly come up with a function in ganga to give you the accessURLs from a list of LFNs.
As you point out that functionality exists with LHCbDirac. You can download a list of LFNs into a text file from Ganga:
```python
J = 1017
j=jobs(J)
thefile = open(str(J)+"_LFNs.txt",'w')
ds = []
for js in j.subjobs:
     if js.status=='completed':
         thefile.write("%s\n" % js.backend.getOutputDataLFNs()[0])
thefile.close()
```
And then point dirac-dms-lfn-accessURL to it:
```
dirac-dms-lfn-accessURL < 1017_LFNs.txt
```

Of course you might end up with two URLs for the same file if it has been replicated. I'll take another look with Ganga in the morning.

Due to padding, I believe adding a single `uint16_t` to the `Timer` class will actually add 4 bytes to each instance.  On the default CLI build for CC2538, that adds 112 bytes RAM (flash overhead difficult to know without some additional prototyping).  For comparison, the change in #1267 adds 16 bytes RAM and 1072 bytes flash.

A separate question to ask: does your use cases for microsecond timer really require a (32-bit millisecond, 16-bit microsecond) representation?  For example, simplifying to a single 32-bit microsecond representation should give you over an hour before roll-over.

A slight variation/view on Option 2 is to just have logically different Timer schedulers (one for millisecond another for microsecond).  If a single 32-bit microsecond representation is sufficient for your use cases, then I think this approach may be preferable because you will not have any increase in RAM overhead.  It also makes clear what timer type is really required by the user, making it obvious which services are affected if a microsecond timer is not provided by a given platform.

Reviewing this issue a bit further, I'm not sure about the possibly bigger problem, which is that the exit status indicates failure in the case that there is any trailing two-space line-break (even though it is not "fixed" unless an additional flag is given - and there is no way to leave it "unfixed" without reporting failure).  That really does feel like a bug to me - but my recollection of the code is pretty clear that the exit status is determined separately from the actual fixing code, and handling the trailing two-space line break specially only in files with markdown extensions would be a bit tricky there (which is why I didn't do that in the first place).  There might need to be a more significant refactoring (of at least the exit status handling in the trailing whitespace plugin) to solve that problem cleanly. Before I get into that, I'd like to know if there is a consensus that either

A. "fixing" plugins should (without any special options) fix anything that would cause a failure (so that after staging automatic changes, a second run would not fail - I'm not sure this is strictly true for existing plugins) meaning that failure should only be indicated when changes have been made (output messages might still be present, although much less likely to be read), or

B. if there should be another (mutually exclusive) option that not only preserves the trailing two-space line break but also suppresses the failure in that case (as there is a chance that the trailing whitespace is not intentional or even functional, depending on the markdown dialect and documentation style in use).

I would kind of lean towards B but am not totally convinced that forcing that additional user interaction (choose explicit 'allow' or 'fix' for trailing two-space line breaks) to prevent recurring failure is really necessary, as leaving the trailing two-space line break without a failure by default is probably going to pass unnoticed and without complaint (even with a non-failure warning about the fact they are not fixed) and has pretty much no functional downside. (I strongly suspect that this issue would never have been opened if the trailing two-space line break had not caused this plugin to report failure and abort the commits.)

Let me explain my problem a bit better:
All tests in test_redis.py access a (single, shared) redis-server instance provided by travis.
Before and after test cases are run, the entire content of the redis database is cleared, so different tests don't affect each other.
This does not work when tests from this file are run concurrently. Using a scope="module" fixture does not help either, because module-level fixtures unfortunately do not affect scheduling (I think this would be #18 ), but are re-instantiated on each worker.

We also do need several classes in the file, because we have a common body of tests we run in a shared superclass, which we then instantiate with different configurations, potentially overriding some tests to adjust for the different configuration. Yet, all of these use the shared redis instance.

In the end, I would agree that this PR is a cheaper workaround around not having #18 implemented, a bit like loadscope is as well.

@bintoro Do you think, `kwargs` should check for existing field name, if not throw an exception ?

```python
class V(Model):
   id = IntType()

v = V(id=1, foo=2)
# Should throw an error `foo` is unknown argument ?
```

Yes, I realize adding `mapping` everywhere is not ideal due to custom types. People should ideally catch unexpected stuff to `*args` and `**kwargs` when creating a custom type but I'm sure not everybody does it.

In my opinion, introspection is quite dirty, may be error prone and not that efficient... So I'd instead go with adding an attribute like `_supportsMappingArg = True` to native types that expect it and checking it with `getattr` when deciding what arguments to pass in the calls. Sure this would still break custom types expecting the `mapping` arg, but I find it very unlikely anyone uses it and if they do, they sure have at least some tests covering deserialization and will find out when upgrading the library.

If you'd rather prefer going with introspection though, fine with me!
Personally, while I don't mind `meta`, I would say I prefer the specificity of `metadata`. Even at twice the character count, it's far from the longest keyword name for fields.

Btw, if we strip the mapping-like methods from `Model`, I don't see a problem with providing a mixin that restores the functionality and more.

Declaring
```python
class BaseModel(Model, MappingMixin):
  pass
```
would
* equip descendant classes with `keys()`, `items()`, `update()`, `pop()`, etc. — the whole enchilada
* register `BaseModel` with the `Mapping` ABC to enable `isinstance` and `issubclass` tests
* raise an error if a model definition attempted to mask any of the mixin methods.

@aedicted how large is each segment? Must be pretty big, 40-45MB each? 

The error you are getting is when the download buffer is empty, it only waits a certain amount of time (60 seconds) for there to be more data - I'd recommend trying with fewer threads and see if that helps. 

This settings is controlled by `--stream-timeout` which has a default value of 60 seconds. If you are downloading 10 segments at a time, 45MB each, 450MB ... that _should_ finish within 60 seconds - assuming your connection is fast enough. If it goes slow for one segment you might have problems, or if a download stalls you _will_ have problems. I think this might actually be the same problem as #851 (I'm not sure @back-to was absolutely correct with the timeouts because it could just go slow or be stalled). You could try setting `--stream-timeout 600` and see if that allows you to download the stream. 

I'm working on a new mechanism for downloading segmented streams that will improve this and would make it easier to retry stalled downloads.

...Err, it isn't as simple as it might appear... 
**media-autobuild_suite** only supports building the latest snapshot 
from the master branch, currently at n3.4-dev. 
 Since I am on Vista x86, I have only installed the 32bit toolchain 
(and built only win32 executables); plus, cmake provided by the MSYS2 toolchain 
is Win7+ only, so I had to do [this](https://github.com/jb-alvarado/media-autobuild_suite/issues/507#issuecomment-296303081). 
The 64bit toolchain can also build 32bit binaries, possibly quicker...

I first built all the needed external libs (see Configuration.txt inside my package) 
by running the suite via media-autobuild_suite.bat; 
the "build/media-autobuild_suite.ini" config file is created according to user's selections 
when first executing that .bat, before subsequent runs it can be edited accordingly... 
Be sure to select the 32bit architecture and WinXP compat flags.
A custom [ffmpeg_options.txt](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/files/990679/ffmpeg_options.txt) was put inside build dir; you can add/subtract libs as desired...

When all needed libs were successfully compiled and installed 
and the suite proceeded to check for ffmpeg-git updates, I killed 
the minnty console (or you can leave it running to compile the 
latest ffmpeg-git version). 

1. 3.3 source was extracted to build/ffmpeg-3.3
2. A new folder was created, build/ffmpeg-3.3/build-static-32bit
Compilation of 3.3 was done purely via minnty console, manually:  
3. Remove ffmpeg stuff (if it exists) from prior compilations:
```
rm -rf $LOCALDESTDIR/include/libavutil
rm -rf $LOCALDESTDIR/include/libavcodec
rm -rf $LOCALDESTDIR/include/libpostproc
rm -rf $LOCALDESTDIR/include/libswresample
rm -rf $LOCALDESTDIR/include/libswscale
rm -rf $LOCALDESTDIR/include/libavdevice
rm -rf $LOCALDESTDIR/include/libavfilter
rm -rf $LOCALDESTDIR/include/libavformat
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/libavutil.a
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/libswresample.a
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/libswscale.a
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/libavcodec.a
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/libavdevice.a
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/libavfilter.a
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/libavformat.a
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/libpostproc.a
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/pkgconfig/libavcodec.pc
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/pkgconfig/libavutil.pc
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/pkgconfig/libpostproc.pc
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/pkgconfig/libswresample.pc
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/pkgconfig/libswscale.pc
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/pkgconfig/libavdevice.pc
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/pkgconfig/libavfilter.pc
rm -f $LOCALDESTDIR/lib/pkgconfig/libavformat.pc
```
4. cd build/ffmpeg-3.3/build-static-32bit
5. Run the following ffmpeg configure (see Configuration.txt): 
```
../configure --arch=x86 --target-os=mingw32 --prefix=$LOCALDESTDIR --bindir=$LOCALDESTDIR/bin-video --pkg-config-flags=--static --enable-fontconfig --enable-libass --enable-libbs2b --enable-libfreetype --enable-libfribidi --enable-libsoxr --enable-libmp3lame --enable-libvorbis --disable-encoder=vorbis --enable-libspeex --enable-libopus --enable-libgsm --enable-libilbc --enable-libtwolame --enable-libwavpack --enable-libxvid --enable-libtheora --enable-libvpx --enable-libx264 --enable-libopenh264 --enable-libx265 --enable-libxavs --enable-libkvazaar --enable-libzvbi --enable-libopenjpeg --enable-libwebp --enable-gnutls --enable-libssh --enable-librtmp --enable-gpl --enable-version3 --disable-ffplay --disable-sdl2 --disable-ffprobe --disable-debug --disable-shared --enable-doc --extra-libs='-lgomp -lpng' --extra-cflags='-DLIBTWOLAME_STATIC -DLIBSSH_STATIC -DKVZ_STATIC_LIB -fopenmp -msse -mfpmath=sse' --optflags='-O2 -finline-functions' --extra-ldflags='-Wl,--allow-multiple-definition'
```
6. Apply some "cosmetics" 
`sed -i "s|--target-os=mingw32 --prefix=$LOCALDESTDIR --bindir=$LOCALDESTDIR/bin-video --pkg-config-flags=--static ||g" config.h`
7. Start compiling: 
`make`
(you may want to reduce CPU cores by one, say `make -j1` for a 2 Core CPU)
8. Install things: 
`make install`
9. You'll find (hopefully) the compiled ffmpeg.exe binary inside "/local32/bin-video" dir.

Hope it's now clear to you...

I am a bit against not doing, what the logging documentation says about logging in a library (https://docs.python.org/3/howto/logging.html#configuring-logging-for-a-library) - and checking for the list of defined handlers seems a but too "low level digging into the logging library" for me.
The "problem" is a bit, that we are library and application developer in the same time. So, after the logging documentation and various blogs I have found in the internet, we should do the following:

In the library code part (this is, e.g. in feature_selector.py), we should go with the NullHandler, as described here (https://docs.python.org/3/howto/logging.html#configuring-logging-for-a-library) and as @moritzgelb implemented it. This behaviour is needed for python versions below 3.2 (read the paragraph above the page I have quoted), but we are using all python versions, so we will need it.
In the application part (e.g. the convenience functions, that most people will use and maybe also the transformers), we should call
```
import logging
logging.basicConfig()
```
once in the beginning. This will not override the settings of the user, if he has defined one.

Also, reading through https://fangpenlin.com/posts/2012/08/26/good-logging-practice-in-python/, we should probably not get the logger on module level, but at a point we will need it.

@marcomusy Regarding hand sizes, in my experience of teaching piano, hand size only affects the fingering choice in rare circumstances. Mostly cause if someone's hands are very small (like a child's hand), then they simply don't play songs that require larger hands. And of course, if there was a significant difference, you could create multiple cost databases, just with different weights for each size. 
And the 1 and 5 fingers shouldn't be discouraged from playing black keys always. It's only in certain circumstances. Indeed, playing octaves on black keys is often done with _only_ 1st and 5th fingers. So if the b-factor is circumstantially aware of when to add the cost and when not to, then you're probably fine. But if it's a global cost, then it will improve some situations, while necessarily making others worse. 

Technical implementations aside, the real need is just to have correct fingerings. Bach pieces like you have are great test pieces, because the correct fingerings are well known and freely available. They're also on the easier side. They don't include the complications of how to handle fingerings with pedals, since most bach pieces have little to no pedal work. 
So if you test it with a few bach pieces and a few other well known pieces, and the accuracy is high (I would think like > 95% the same as official fingerings), then you've likely got a solid algorithm. I say all this only to be helpful in making sure we have a quality module for music21. Cheers - Blake

@istoica @imzhenyu I would explain my scheme in this comment.

For task execution chain in an Actor:

T1 -> S1 -> T2 -> S2 -> ... -> T_m -> S_m -> ... -> T_n -> S_n (checkpoint) -> ...,

we use **previous** and **next** to describe the order of these tasks and states. For instance, `T1` is **previous** to `T_m`, and `S2` is the **next** state of `T2`.

My scheme is based on the fact that given a task `T_m` and the first checkpoint `S_n` next to it, if all the tasks between `T_m` (included) and `S_n` are marked as `GCed` (i.e. all of there batches have ended), `T_m` would **never** be re-executed due to lineage reconstruction along this task execution chain, but it **may** be re-executed due to lineage reconstruction along task execution chain of another Actor.

My scheme is described below:

-  When a batch ends, the tasks and objects of this batch would be marked as `GCed` instead of being deleted immediately.
- When an checkpoint is put, we traverse tasks backwardly from the task which generates this checkpoint to **previous** tasks one by one.
- During the traversal, when we reach a task which is not marked as `GCed`, we stop traversing and mark this task as `ACTING`. 
- After an `ACTING` task being marked as `GCed` in the future, the traversal continues from it.
- When a traversal reaches a checkpoint, it could stops.
- After all the tasks of a batch has been traversed, the objects and tasks of this batch could be deleted safely.

No docs on this exist, sorry. But it is simple:

In the root of your repo, create a shell/python script called `jenkins.sh` or `jenkins.py` that performs the steps you want executed on continuous integration. It is important that the script fails with a non-zero exit code if the tests or any other build step fails.

The script will be sourced, so it should not have a shebang at the beginning.

You may also need a setup.py. This is standard for Python packages, and well documented. 

https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/

WRT native dependencies, I'm assuming you only need Python 2.7.x and Docker. If you need Toil outside of a container, `jenkins.sh` should install the required version of Toil in a virtualenv and then invoke `python setup.py develop` for your 

Look at the toil-rnaseq, toil-scripts or toil-lib projects for inspiration. You might want to track this as an issue that you can assign to me once you have all the build infrastructure files in place.

A Makefile is recommended but not required.

Okay, I had time to dig a bit deeper. `get_ancestors_and_self` isn't expensive in itself, it's the fact that with @mvantellingen's data set we call it 1900 times because there's 1900 categories at level 0 and 1! You wouldn't do that in real life.

In `nav_primary.html`, `category_tree` gets called with a max_depth of 2, because we used to have a dropdown submenu. That doesn't seem to be working any more, I only get the root categories. Hence, we could lower the depth to `1` there, and things work as expected.

Furthermore, there's of course still some inefficiency in calling `get_absolute_url` for each and every category, if we're constructing the entire tree and have knowledge about how we build the URLs. If we want to break that separation of concerns, it's entirely feasible to be able to even manage those silly numbers of categories. 

As it stands, I don't consider it a blocker for a release. But I have some time on my flight, and I might toy with passing in the parent slug to `get_absolute_url` to make sure it doesn't incur another database hit.

It is a relatevely simple task as the Oscar uses [dynamic class loading](https://django-oscar.readthedocs.io/en/latest/topics/class_loading_explained.html#dynamic-class-loading-explained), so you have to [fork](https://django-oscar.readthedocs.io/en/latest/topics/customisation.html#fork-the-oscar-app) appropriate dashboard app, and.. 

>  All you usually need to do is give it the same name and place it in a module with the same name.

Earlier versions of Oscar had contains a Demo site which was a good tutorial for that kind of tasks, try to grep over old releases :)

For example, we have customised Product model and have added several fields. And we want to show it in the form for editing. So, we create the file `apps/dashboard/catalogue/forms.py` which contains something like that:
```
from oscar.apps.dashboard.catalogue import forms as base_forms

class ProductForm(base_forms.ProductForm):

    class Meta(base_forms.ProductForm.Meta):

        fields = [
            'title', 'upc', 'on_sale', 
            'short_description', 'description',
            'out_of_stock', 'bestseller',
            'is_new', 'is_discountable', 'structure',
            'markdown', 'markdown_reason', ]
```
Restart server and... that's all, folks :)
Be sure you have told to Oscar where to find your classes ([via](https://django-oscar.readthedocs.io/en/latest/topics/customisation.html#replace-oscar-s-app-with-your-own-in-installed-apps) `get_core_apps(['apps.dashboard.catalogue'])` in the settings module)

That's an interesting argument, and one we should consider more closely.  Even reasoning about this i.t.o. a line model, one is tempted to think that a solution can be found much more rapidly if each fit is a least squares fit; that is, *if* you are lucky enough not to accidentally draw outliers.  There are two extremes: draw as few samples as possible, minimizing the chance of drawing outliers, or drawing as many data points as possible (least squares).  Clearly, we want to stay close to the lower boundary, but the question is how close.

From http://homepages.inf.ed.ac.uk/rbf/CVonline/LOCAL_COPIES/CANTZLER2/ransac.pdf:

"Drawing more than the minimal number of sample points is inefficient, since the probability of selecting a sample consisting only of inlying data points (i.e. all data points belonging to the same model), that gives a good estimate and at random, decreases with respect to the increasing sample size.  Thus the minimal sample set maximizes the probability of selecting a set of inliers from which later a good estimate will be computed."

Perhaps we should consider another question: do we have the correct metric for inliers?  Because that is what determines whether a model is *valid* or not, and if a pathological fit is considered valid, clearly we have a problem.

I think there are broadly two somewhat competing interests. On one side, you might want to capture self or cls (for classmethods) in the patched version - e.g. to be sure a particular subclass is in use. On the other side, being able to write regular predictable code is useful : but we're doing something intrinsically unusual - and patching C.foo vs C().foo is unfortunately intrinsic complexity.


Here's a bit of as-complex-as-I-can-imagine code:

```
class C:
    def __init__(self, ...):
        self.thing = something
    def foo(self):
        pass

class MyTest(TestCase):
    def replacement(self, c):
        self.captured_thing = c.thing
    def test_foo(self):
        with MonkeyPatch('C.foo', self.replacement):
            ....some code that triggers a C to be made
        self.assertEqual(42, self.captured_thing)
```

here, we're patching a bound method onto an unbound method, and desiring that the original callable 's additional parameters be the parameters for the unbound method - so 'c' for replacement becomes 'self 'for foo.

I don't know if that makes sense as a programmer model: basically saying 'the thing you pass in should be callable and the args you'd pass it are the args the existing callsite requires - so to call self.replacement above requires 1 parameter, and the existing callsite requires one parameter.

Lets see how that might pan out for the various permutations:

| callsite | staticmethod | classmethod | function | boundmethod | unboundmethod | 
|----------|--------------------|----------------- | -----------|--------------------|------------------------|
|@staticmethod def foo()| @staticmethod def foo() | @classmethod def foo(cls) | def foo() | def foo(self) | incompatible [source can't be called, target may not be called with an instance] |
|@classmethod def foo(cls) | @staticmethod def foo(cls) | @classmethod def foo(cls, targetcls) | def foo(cls) | def foo(self, cls) | def foo(cls) # confusing, but we could make it work if we want to |
|def foo() | @staticmethod def foo() | @classmethod def foo(cls) | def foo() | def foo(self) | incompatible | 
| (bound) def foo(self) | @staticmethod def foo() | @classmethod def foo(cls) | def foo() | def foo(self) | def foo(targetself) # again confusing if it worked but we could choose to make it
| (unbound) def foo(self) | @staticmethod def foo(targetself) | @classmethod def foo(cls, targetself) | def foo(targetself) | def foo(self, targetself) | def foo(targetself) # perhaps still confusing, but at least more obvious? |

So to me that seems like a pretty plausible table - if we say 'patching in an unbound method is unsupported' then we get rid of the inconsistent corner cases and it becomes simply - the thing you patch in is a callable, and it will be called as if you'd taken the callable, and then called it with all the parameters the original target has written.

What do you think? [I realise this may not work without modifying Nova, but I think that that is secondary to getting a good answer down here in the plumbing].

What I've done now is to just create a new function which captures the method to be called in a closure. This is functionally equivalent to using the PartialUnboundDescriptor, or creating a new types.FunctionType, but benefits from being simpler to construct. What I realized while trying to create a types.FunctionType object is that creating a wrapper function would be necessary to construct the FunctionType to do what is necessary, but at that point there's no need to take the further step of manually creating the FunctionType.

The upshot is that this is essentially the exact same as PartialUnboundMethod it's just that the original method is captured in a closure rather than being set on the descriptor class. So I'm happy to go this route or convert back to the descriptor.

Very interesting, I didn't know about this!  I think it's growing less and less likely that people are using <3.0 for CMake, but 2.8.12 still seems to be a default.  So maybe include the small blurb first just up to `targe_link_libraries`, and then say "If, however, you are using CMake less than 3.0, you also need to ... `include_directories` example"?

Part of the reason I never submitted a PR is because the maintainers here need to make a decision on how they want to approach it.  The options I see are

1. Set both, allowing for existing projects that already have it using the "missing 2" version to still function.
2. Set the correct one, adding docs explaining how to support both versions.
3. Fix it, and ignore.

I think (2) is the right approach, but doing either (2) or (3) pushes the problem downstream to any projects currently using `librealsense`.  So at some future point, some project `foo` will have an issue raised saying "I can't compile with `librealsense`", having docs on the main README will hopefully help people solve it faster.

The primary argument for not doing `1` is if anybody rolled out their own `FindLibRealSense.cmake` for **version 1.x**, setting both will potentially create conflicts for them.

`python-docx` is based on the `lxml` package, which is uses to do all the XML manipulation. That means that every element can be manipulated with `lxml` methods, in particular the ones here: http://lxml.de/api/lxml.etree._Element-class.html

It also means that `xml.dom.minidom` has no place here. It does not mix with `lxml` and `lxml` can do whatever you need with the XML.

It sounds like maybe the `.getnext()` method would do what you're looking for. You could just `.getnext()` your way though from the bookmark start until you hit the bookmark end.

> a naive implementation would allow the third party to close the channel at any moment, which is not desirable

Well, how about a more involved approach:
- Alice (A) sends regular updates (U) to the third party (T) 
- T is incentivized to close the channel, in case of #181 by a fee enclosed in U
- T is disincentivized to close the channel under normal operation by a mandatory deposit for third party closing (TPC)
- in case of #181 T is supposed to ~ping A before acting
- A can dispute the TPC during a short time period

Open attack vectors:
- A can pretend to be silent in order to grab the TPC deposit
  - now A would lose reputation and not be served again by T

Ah, now I understand. The `| Click Element | //input... |` keyword is stuck? And when you call the keyword, it also closes the child window, right? This might be problem on Selenium side. Selenium provides a blocking type of API and in practice Selenium listen the `onLoad` event from the browser before it returns (when performing interaction with the elements in the browser). Now if you close the child window before the event is triggered, the Selenium does not never return and therefore the keyword is stuck.

But this is only educated guesses, because I don't see how your application works.  But it certainly looks like that, because in the log file, the timestamp jumps from 10:14:53 to 10:17:13 in the `Click Element` keyword. The goal here is to write a simple but often used model with this functionality.

With a factory function the user interface becomes

```
table_class = lookup_table_model(lookup_table, points, class_name='MyTableModel1D')
table = table_class(lookup_table, points, **kwargs)
```
This approach has a subtle philosophical advantage but in terms of usage goes contrary to the rest of the models without actually changing the model instance in any way.

I think what would be useful is to have a factory function which creates a different `LookupTable` class based on the interpolation algorithm used and not on the dimensions of the table. So unless I see other :+1: for factory function I think it's best to keep it simple.

Thanks for reviewing, @yiheng. I've addressed your comments. Please let me know if there is anything else.

I don't think you need to change the behavior of the Cell wrt `pretopology`.

The `pretopology` is a D x W linear for RNN, and a D x 4W linear for LSTM; and previously `Recurrent` just pass the (B*T) x D input to the `pretopology`, and slice the appropriate portion for each timestep to sent to each cell.

`recurrent Decoder` can instead, for each time step, pass the B x H input to the `pretopology`, and then send the result to each cell.

Hello @zhongboyin,

The option -neg allows you to choose the number of negative samples that are sampled at each iteration when using the negative sampling loss. More negative samples will yield a more accurate approximation of the loss at increased computational cost. Please see following paper for details on this procedure.

[Distributed Representations of Words and Phrases and their Compositionality](https://arxiv.org/abs/1310.4546) by Tomas Mikolov, Ilya Sutskever, Kai Chen, Greg Corrado, Jeffrey Dean

Thanks,

A lot of the fields in marshmallow do not strictly validate the type and attempt to coax the value to the field type for convenience. `Boolean` supports stricter use cases by allowing you to specify the exact values to allow for `True` and `False` using the `truthy` and `falsy` arguments.

https://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/3.0/api_reference.html#marshmallow.fields.Boolean

I have mentioned in a discussion before (can't find a link to it) that this kind of behavior represents an opinion and is not necessarily expected behavior for a validation library.

I think the primitive fields should have strict type enforcement and specialized fields should be used explicitly parse a value from one type to another. We could still offer convenient field types, but their names should communicate the complexity that comes with the convenience. Something like `BooleanAny = Union(Boolean, BooleanString, BooleanNumber)`.

What is happening here is that you are running into the fact that primary_keys are by default in marshmallow-sqlalchemy set to [dump_only](https://marshmallow.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api_reference.html#marshmallow.Schema).

So when you deserialize your data, primary key fields will be silently ignored and set to None.

I've formulated this into something runnable:

```python
import sqlalchemy
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
import marshmallow
from marshmallow_sqlalchemy import ModelSchema, field_for

Base = declarative_base()


class Tariff(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'tariff'

    id = sqlalchemy.schema.Column(sqlalchemy.types.Integer, primary_key=True)
    title = sqlalchemy.schema.Column(sqlalchemy.types.String())

    # Course this tariff belongs to
    course_id = sqlalchemy.schema.Column(sqlalchemy.types.Integer, sqlalchemy.schema.ForeignKey('course.id'))
    course = sqlalchemy.orm.relationship(
        'Course',
        back_populates='tariffs'
    )

    # Price of this payment plan
    price = sqlalchemy.schema.Column(sqlalchemy.types.Integer)


class Course(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'course'

    id = sqlalchemy.schema.Column(sqlalchemy.types.Integer, primary_key=True)
    title = sqlalchemy.schema.Column(sqlalchemy.types.String())

    # levels = sqlalchemy.orm.relationship(
    #     'Level',
    #     back_populates='course'
    # )

    tariffs = sqlalchemy.orm.relationship(
        'Tariff',
        back_populates='course'
    )


engine = sqlalchemy.create_engine('sqlite:///:memory:', echo=False)
Base.metadata.create_all(engine)
Session = sqlalchemy.orm.sessionmaker(bind=engine)
session = Session()


class CourseSchema(ModelSchema):
    class Meta:
        model = Course
        sqla_session = session

    tariffs = marshmallow.fields.Nested('TariffSchema', many=True, exclude=('course',))
    # id = field_for(Course, 'id', dump_only=False)

class TariffSchema(ModelSchema):
    class Meta:
        model = Tariff
        sqla_session = session

    course = marshmallow.fields.Nested(CourseSchema)
    # id = field_for(Tariff, 'id', dump_only=False)

# I removed 'course_id:999' as that does nothing.
j = {"course":{"id":999},"title":"uu","price":"222"}

tariff_obj = TariffSchema().load(j).data
print 'Before Commit', tariff_obj.course_id, tariff_obj.course.id

session.add(tariff_obj)
session.commit()

print 'After Commit', tariff_obj.course_id, tariff_obj.course.id
```

This will output:
```
Before Commit None None
After Commit 1 1
```

I changed the id's in the `j` data to have a `course_id` of `999` so that after the commit it was obvious that this had been ignored in favour of the auto-generated ids.

If you comment out `# id = field_for(Course, 'id', dump_only=False)` then you can make that field be deserialized.

```
Before Commit None 999
After Commit 999 999
```

This is an interesting issue and seems to be an inherent part of python. It's going to take some thinking to figure out how to handle this one.

That said, the reason this error is occurring is because there are duplicate summoners in the list passed to `pool.starmap`. If you remove the duplicates first, the code won't error.

Mostly for myself, here is some code that reproduces the same error in pure python (not using Cass):

```
from multiprocessing.dummy import Pool as ThreadPool

def count():
    i = 0
    while True:
        i += 1
        yield i
c = count()

def load(c):
    return {c.__next__() for _ in range(10000)}

pool = ThreadPool(16)
pool.map(load, [c for _ in range(100)])
pool.close()
pool.join()
```

Note that if you replace `c` with `count()` in the `pool.map` function that the code runs without erroring. This is because a different generator is being passed to `load` within each of the threads.

```
is_async = settings.PSQ_ASYNC_QUEUES

if is_async:

fast = psq.Queue(pubsub=pubsub_client, name="fast", async=is_async)
slow = psq.Queue(pubsub=pubsub_client, name="slow", async=is_async)
```

vs.

```
is_async = settings.PSQ_ASYNC_QUEUES

if is_async:
    args = dict(pubsub_client=google.cloud.pubsub.Client())
    QueueClass = psq.Queue
else:
    args = dict()
    QueueClass = psq.ImmediateQueue

fast = QueueClass(name="fast", **args)
slow = QueueClass(name="slow", **args)
```

(Given that you'd want to not copy the actual queues' definitions)

rq took the first approach, which is also what nudged me into this direction. But honestly, I can live with both solutions, and don't have a strong opinion. @ajtritt 
> By 'bells and whistles' do you mean extensions? Do you mean user defined classes that extend the existing pynwb classes?"

yes. perhaps a better example is the inverse (or is it the converse?)

Let's say I have a file someone gave me that was created with an extended specification. We'll call this extended specification `bells_and_whistles` ... it has some extensions... perhaps it has some attributes that are marked as "required" that are optional is the core spec?

pynwb should handle this easily by default

``` Python
# discover spec from `bells_and_whistles.nwb` file
from pynwb.io import HDF5IO
io2 = HDF5IO('bells_and_whistles.nwb', 'r')
```

However, let's say I have an analysis package or visualization package that works only on the core specification. I should be able to explicitly tell HDF5IO which specification I want to use.

``` Python
# open `bells_and_whistles.nwb` file with core specification
from pynwb.io import HDF5IO
from pynwb.specifications import core
io2 = HDF5IO('bells_and_whistles.nwb', 'r', specification=core)
```

Maybe my analysis package doesn't actually want to use the core specification, but rather sits "in between" `core` and `bells_and_whistles`... e.g. it wants to enforce certain required attributes, but is still unaware of the extensions in `bells_and_whistles`

``` Python
# open `bells_and_whistles.nwb` file with 'just_the_bells` specification
from pynwb.io import HDF5IO
from my_package.specifications import just_the_bells
io2 = HDF5IO('bells_and_whistles.nwb', 'r', specification=just_the_bells)
```
